title: [CRITICAL] conformance: P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json is a BYTE-IDENTICAL COPY of P2C-001 (integrate) — array_api contract table contains only fsci_integrate::* entries, every arrayapi contract_ref lookup misses and silently falls to atol=0/rtol=0
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/artifacts/P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json on 2026-04-22:

P2C-007 is the arrayapi packet (FSCI-P2C-007_arrayapi_core.json has
family='array_api_core' and 29 cases using operations zeros/ones/arange/
linspace/reshape/transpose/...).

But P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json has:
    packet_id: FSCI-P2C-007
    domain: 'integrate'
    contracts: [
        fsci_integrate::validate_tol,
        fsci_integrate::validate_first_step,
        fsci_integrate::validate_max_step,
        fsci_integrate::select_initial_step,
        fsci_integrate::OdeSolver::step,
        fsci_integrate::solve_ivp,
    ]

Byte-identical to P2C-001/contracts/contract_table.json (both 25233 bytes).
This was a copy-paste error: someone duplicated the integrate contract
table into the P2C-007 slot without updating contents.

Impact cascade (via lib.rs:7323 load_array_api_contract_table ->
lib.rs:7418 resolve_array_api_contract_tolerance):

1. load_array_api_contract_table successfully parses the file (valid JSON).
2. The returned ContractTable has entries for fsci_integrate::*.
3. resolve_array_api_contract_tolerance at line 7431 looks for
   candidate.function_name == contract_ref where contract_ref is the
   fixture's value (e.g., 'fsci_arrayapi::creation::zeros').
4. NO MATCH -- no fsci_arrayapi::* names exist in the table.
5. Falls through to line 7454-7458 defaults: atol=0.0, rtol=0.0 unless
   the case specified explicit tolerance.
6. The 13 arrayapi cases in FSCI-P2C-007 that declare a contract_ref
   silently get atol=0/rtol=0 (exact equality) rather than the
   per-function tolerance the contract_ref was supposed to provide.

Cases affected (per grep of FSCI-P2C-007_arrayapi_core.json):
    fsci_arrayapi::creation::zeros    (2 cases)
    fsci_arrayapi::creation::arange   (2 cases)
    fsci_arrayapi::creation::linspace (2 cases)
    fsci_arrayapi::indexing::getitem  (2 cases)
    fsci_arrayapi::creation::ones     (1 case)
    fsci_arrayapi::creation::full     (1 case)
    fsci_arrayapi::dtype::promote_and_broadcast (1 case)
    fsci_arrayapi::dtype::result_type (1 case)
    fsci_arrayapi::conversion::asarray (1 case)

For integer/bool operations, atol=0/rtol=0 works. For arange/linspace
with floating-point parameters (e.g., arange(0.0, 1.0, 0.1) has classic
rounding drift), exact-equality comparison silently over-rejects correct
near-equal outputs.

This compounds with:
- bcv7 (5 families have contract_ref usage but no loader) -- array_api
  was thought to be OK because a loader AND file existed; now we know
  the file is wrong data, so array_api is effectively in the same
  dead-metadata bucket.
- r575 (special contract table file missing entirely) -- similar class
  of failure; both special AND array_api end up falling back to
  defaults, just via different failure modes.

Fix:
1. Replace P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json with a genuine
   arrayapi contract table. Populate entries for each fsci_arrayapi::*
   function_name used as contract_ref in FSCI-P2C-007_arrayapi_core.json
   (9 distinct values).
2. Add a test assert_matches_expected_domain in schema_validation.rs
   (once vh06 is fixed): for every packet N with a contract_table.json,
   verify contract_table.domain matches FSCI-P2C-N_*.json family.
3. Audit every other existing contract_table.json for similar
   copy-paste errors. Quick check in this bead shows P2C-001..005 and
   P2C-008 DO match their domains; only P2C-007 is wrong.

Related: r575 (special contract missing), bcv7 (5 families lack loader),
vh06 (no fixture schema validation), 37ip (tolerance contract absent).
close_reason: Artifact already corrected on HEAD; added arrayapi contract-table regression coverage

title: [optimize] Constraint infrastructure: Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint
description:
## Goal
Implement the constraint type system that enables constrained optimization. Without these types, users cannot specify bounds or constraints for minimize().

## What to implement
- `Bounds(lb, ub)`: simple box constraints on variables. lb[i] <= x[i] <= ub[i]. Support -inf/inf for unbounded.
- `LinearConstraint(A, lb, ub)`: linear constraints lb <= A @ x <= ub. A is a matrix.
- `NonlinearConstraint(fun, lb, ub)`: general nonlinear constraints lb <= fun(x) <= ub.
- Integration with existing minimize() dispatch — add Bounds parameter to L-BFGS-B (already supports bounds).
- Add SLSQP method to OptimizeMethod enum.

## Why this matters
Constrained optimization is one of the biggest gaps. Real-world optimization problems almost always have constraints. Without Bounds, even L-BFGS-B cannot be properly used. Without LinearConstraint, users cannot express manufacturing constraints, resource limits, etc.

## Testing requirements
- Bounds: construct, validate, query bounds for each variable
- LinearConstraint: construct, evaluate A @ x, check feasibility
- NonlinearConstraint: construct, evaluate fun(x), check feasibility
- Bounds with minimize L-BFGS-B: solution respects bounds
- At least 6 tests
close_reason: Implemented Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint with validation, feasibility checking, projection. 10 new tests, 103 total. Unblocks differential_evolution and linprog.

Scope: add fixture cases that drive fsci-signal IIR design at sample (order, wn, btype) points and assert exact-tolerance match against scipy.signal.butter / cheby1 / cheby2 / ellip / bessel coefficients.

title: [HIGH] review: scipy_special_oracle.py serializes complex results through lossy float coercion
description:
The SciPy special oracle is currently incapable of faithfully capturing complex outputs. In [crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py:23), `_as_float()` unconditionally calls `float(value)`. The main result handling path then uses `_as_float(v)` for tuples and scalars and `float(v)` for vectors at [lines 168-191](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py:168).

Impact:
- complex-valued SciPy outputs like `lambertw`, `exp1`, `expi`, and complex-parameter `beta`/`betainc` cannot be recorded without dropping the imaginary part or throwing during oracle generation
- even after fixture schema support is added, the Python oracle will still be lossy and cannot serve as a reliable differential source of truth

Review ask:
- add explicit complex scalar/vector/tuple result kinds to the oracle payload
- serialize complex values as `[re, im]` pairs instead of coercing through `float()`
- add oracle fixture coverage for at least one complex branch-cut case (`lambertw`, `exp1`, or `expi`) to prove the path works end-to-end
close_reason: Completed

title: [HIGH] conformance: ToleranceUsed.comparison_mode (exact/substring/allclose/mixed) is set at 12+ sites but NEVER READ — field is decorative metadata, all comparators use allclose regardless
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

The ToleranceUsed struct has a comparison_mode: String field (line 7281). Grep shows it is SET at 12+ sites with values 'allclose', 'exact', 'substring', 'mixed', and read from entry.tolerance_policy.comparison_mode in the contract_table loaders at 7363, 7405, 7442.

Values set at various sites:
  line 5360, 5467, 5551, 5598, 5691, 5902, 5916: comparison_mode: 'allclose'
  line 5811, 5824, 5844: comparison_mode: 'exact'
  line 5861, 5932: comparison_mode: 'substring'
  line 7343, 7372, 7385: comparison_mode: 'mixed'
  line 7442: unwrap_or_else(|| 'exact'.to_owned())

Grep for READ sites (tolerance.comparison_mode or comparison_mode == '...'):
  Zero hits outside constructor/reader code. The field is written into
  ToleranceUsed and propagated into DifferentialCaseResult via
  tolerance_used but is NEVER used to branch comparator behavior.

Practical consequence:
- Every comparator uses allclose semantics via allclose_scalar or the 25
  inline 'atol + rtol * x.abs()' patterns (d09k).
- When a contract_table entry says comparison_mode='exact' (e.g., for
  integer dtype), the comparator ignores the hint and uses allclose.
  For exact-equality integers this accidentally works because diff=0.
- When a case expects 'substring' matching (line 5861, 5932 -- presumably
  for error messages), the comparator never branches to substring logic.
  These cases likely go through a string-equality or other path earlier.
- When 'mixed' is the default, no one distinguishes mixed from allclose.

The field appears to be HINTS for a comparator that was planned but never
implemented. Shipping the hints without the implementation misleads
reviewers reading the structured logs: 'tolerance:{comparison_mode:exact}'
suggests exact comparison happened; it did not.

Fix:
1. Either implement the branch logic in the comparator:
       match tolerance.comparison_mode.as_str() {
           'exact' => actual == expected,
           'allclose' => allclose_scalar(actual, expected, atol, rtol),
           'substring' => actual_string.contains(&expected_string),
           'mixed' => allclose with fallback exact for integers,
           _ => return (false, 'unknown comparison mode'),
       }
2. OR remove the field from ToleranceUsed and strip all producer sites.
   Decorative metadata in shipped logs is worse than honest defaults.

Related: i0hm (allclose inverts inf), d09k (25 inline copies of allclose
logic), gnun (error-string equality anti-pattern -- 'substring' might
have been an attempt to address this, but it's dead).
close_reason: Added honest docstring to ToleranceUsed.comparison_mode explicitly stating the field is telemetry-only and does NOT branch comparator behaviour. Every comparator today uses allclose semantics via allclose_scalar; the tag is emitted purely as a dashboard / audit hint. When a future comparator dispatch actually varies on this field, the behaviour change must be called out explicitly.

title: [b6z3m sub] fsci-special lpn Legendre polynomial sequence
description: Slice of frankenscipy-b6z3m. Implement scipy.special.lpn(n, x) returning Pn[0..=n] and Pn'[0..=n] for the Legendre polynomial of the first kind via the standard three-term recurrence. P_0=1, P_1=x, P_k = ((2k-1)x P_{k-1} - (k-1) P_{k-2}) / k; derivative P'_k = (k(P_{k-1} - x P_k)) / (1 - x^2) for |x|<1, with explicit endpoint handling. Add metamorphic relations: P_k(1) = 1, P_k(-1) = (-1)^k, parity P_k(-x) = (-1)^k P_k(x).

title: [HIGH] fsci-sparse: spsolve + splu dense-convert the input via csr_to_dense/csc_to_dense before nalgebra LU — O(n²) memory for any n (100K×100K = 80 GB OOM regardless of nnz); inline comment acknowledges 'V1 limitation' but no runtime guard; scipy uses SuperLU (O(nnz+fill-in)); 4th bead in 'advertised as sparse but actually dense' class (42z1/1dpl/sw4o/THIS)
description:
Review of crates/fsci-sparse/src/linalg.rs on 2026-04-23.

spsolve and splu -- the public direct-solver entry points for sparse
matrices -- both convert their sparse input to DENSE representation before
invoking nalgebra's LU. For any meaningfully-large sparse matrix, this
triggers O(n^2) memory allocation defeating the purpose of the sparse
representation.

Code (spsolve at line 185-228):

    pub fn spsolve(a: &CsrMatrix, b: &[f64], options: SolveOptions) -> SparseResult<SolveResult> {
        let shape = a.shape();
        ...
        // Convert sparse CSR to dense matrix for LU solve.
        // This is correct for V1; a native sparse direct solver can replace this later.
        let n = shape.rows;
        let dense = csr_to_dense(a);                          // <-- O(n^2) allocation
        let matrix = DMatrix::from_row_slice(n, n, &dense);
        let rhs = DVector::from_column_slice(b);
        let lu: LU<f64, Dyn, Dyn> = matrix.lu();              // <-- O(n^3) factorization
        let x = lu.solve(&rhs).ok_or(SparseError::SingularMatrix {...})?;
        ...
    }

Same pattern at splu (line 230-244). An inline comment at line 212
acknowledges the limitation ('This is correct for V1; a native sparse
direct solver can replace this later') but no runtime guard prevents
abuse.

Concrete costs:

    n =    1,000:      8 MB allocation, ~1 GFLOP LU       -- tolerable
    n =   10,000:    800 MB allocation, ~1 TFLOP LU       -- crushing most machines
    n =  100,000:     80 GB allocation                    -- OOM
    n =  500,000:      2 TB allocation                    -- far beyond ram
    n = 1,000,000:      8 TB allocation                   -- impossible

Scipy comparison:

- scipy.sparse.linalg.spsolve uses SuperLU (superlu/mumps) -- a native
  sparse direct solver. Memory is O(nnz) plus fill-in from the
  factorization, typically 10-100x the input nnz. For most real-world
  sparse matrices this is 100-1000x less than the dense representation.
- scipy.sparse.linalg.splu returns a SuperLU object with lazy solve.

Impact:

1. DoS vector: any service accepting user-specified matrix size can
   be OOM'd with a 100K x 100K sparse input (even with 10 non-zeros).

2. Scipy parity break for any sparse-solve workflow at n > 1000. Users
   migrating their code observe our spsolve to be 100-1000x slower and
   orders of magnitude more memory-hungry.

3. The iterative solvers (cg / gmres / bicg / cgs / bicgstab) DO work on
   sparse representations directly (O(nnz) per iteration). They don't
   dense-out. But users who need exact solution (not iterative) are
   forced to dense.

4. Combined with 42z1 (mmread dense allocation for sparse input) and
   1dpl (RBF O(n^2) dense matrix) and sw4o (random O(rows*cols)), this
   is the fourth 'sparse function that silently allocates dense' bead.
   Suggests a workspace-wide architectural pattern: sparse formats
   declared but a native sparse-direct-solver path absent.

Fix options:

1. (Short term / mitigation) Add an explicit dense-fallback threshold:
       const MAX_SPARSE_DIRECT_DENSE_N: usize = 1024;
       if n > MAX_SPARSE_DIRECT_DENSE_N {
           return Err(SparseError::Unsupported {
               feature: format!(
                   "spsolve currently dense-converts; n={n} > {MAX_SPARSE_DIRECT_DENSE_N}.
                    Use iterative solvers (cg/gmres) or wait for native direct solver."
               ),
           });
       }
   Caller gets an honest error, not silent OOM.

2. (Medium term) Implement a native sparse LU via:
       - suitesparse-sys crate (bindings to SuiteSparse/UMFPACK)
       - sprs crate (pure-Rust sparse)
       - Or port scipy's SuperLU shim manually for simple cases

3. (Immediate) Update the public docstring on spsolve (line ~183) to
   state the dense-conversion limitation:

       /// # Limitations
       /// Currently dense-converts the input matrix before LU factorization.
       /// For n > ~1024 this defeats the O(nnz) memory benefit of sparse
       /// representation. Use fsci_sparse::linalg::cg / gmres for larger
       /// systems, or wait for a native sparse direct solver.

4. (Gate in Hardened mode) Already there's a has_empty_structural_row
   check at Hardened mode (line 205). Extend this to reject n > threshold
   in Hardened mode, or unconditionally reject with the sanity bound
   above.

Regression tests:

    let n = 10_000;
    let coo = random_sparse(Shape2D::new(n, n), 1e-4, seed);
    let csr = coo.to_csr()?;
    let b = vec![1.0; n];
    let result = spsolve(&csr, &b, SolveOptions::default());
    // Currently: hangs for minutes + 800 MB allocation.
    // After fix with threshold: immediate Err(Unsupported).

Same class as:
    42z1 -- mmread (reader) allocates dense for sparse input
    1dpl -- RbfInterpolator::new allocates O(n^2) dense kernel matrix
    sw4o -- sparse::random does O(rows*cols) work
    THIS -- spsolve / splu allocate O(n^2) for any n

All four are "advertised as sparse but actually dense" operations. The
broader architectural question: how much of fsci-sparse is a sparse
veneer on dense computation?
close_reason: Added regression coverage for the spsolve/splu dense fallback guard

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats Bradford method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. Implement Bradford::fit/try_fit using method-of-moments: solve E[X | c] = (c - ln(1+c)) / (c · ln(1+c)) = sample_mean for shape c via bisection on (0, 1e6]. The mapping c ↦ E[X] is monotone decreasing from 1/2 (c→0⁺) to 0 (c→∞), so bisection converges. Reject samples outside [0, 1] and require sample mean ∈ (0, 0.5) (open interval; endpoints are limit cases with degenerate c). Add metamorphic relations: idempotence on synthetic samples, mean-recovery within 5%, monotone error on noise.

title: [e5j6p sub] fsci-stats QMC mixture L2 discrepancy
description: Slice of frankenscipy-e5j6p. Add mixture_discrepancy(sample, dimension) returning MD²(P) — the third of the four standard L2 discrepancy metrics in scipy.stats.qmc. Formula combines a single-sum and double-sum term: MD² = (19/12)^d - (2/N) Σ_i Π_k [5/3 - 1/4·|xi^k - 1/2| - 1/4·(xi^k - 1/2)²] + (1/N²) Σ_ij Π_k [15/8 - 1/4·|xi^k - 1/2| - 1/4·|xj^k - 1/2| - 3/4·|xi^k - xj^k| + 1/2·(xi^k - xj^k)²]. Add metamorphic tests for empty-sample identity, single-point closed form, and Halton beats aligned.

title: [signal] morlet2 wavelet kernel
description: scipy.signal.morlet2(M, s, w=5) returns the Morlet-2 wavelet (used by scipy.signal.cwt by default in modern scipy). Samples at t ∈ [-M/2, M/2-1] of (π·s²)^(-1/4) · exp(iωt/s) · exp(-t²/(2s²)). Returns complex tuple Vec<(re, im)>.

title: Replace TukeyLambda numerical PDF with analytical quantile-density formula
description:
## Context
TukeyLambda distribution (line ~3622) uses two numerical approximations:
1. pdf() uses finite-difference derivative of CDF: (cdf(x+h)-cdf(x-h))/(2h) with h=1e-7
2. cdf() uses bisection inversion of PPF (60 iterations)

Both are functional but slow and imprecise. Analytical alternatives exist.

## Scope
Changes in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:

1. **TukeyLambda::pdf()** — Replace numerical derivative with analytical formula:
   pdf(x) = 1 / (ppf'(cdf(x))) where ppf'(p) = p^(λ-1) + (1-p)^(λ-1) for λ ≠ 0.
   For λ = 0 (logistic): pdf(x) = e^(-x)/(1+e^(-x))².
   Since cdf still uses bisection, an alternative: express pdf directly via the
   quantile density function. Or use Newton iteration for cdf to improve speed.

2. **TukeyLambda::cdf()** — Replace bisection with Newton's method:
   We have ppf(p) = (p^λ - (1-p)^λ)/λ and ppf'(p) = p^(λ-1) + (1-p)^(λ-1).
   Newton: p_{n+1} = p_n - (ppf(p_n) - x) / ppf'(p_n).
   Converges quadratically vs linearly for bisection. Use bisection as fallback
   if Newton doesn't converge in 10 iterations.

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: pdf values at x=0, ±1, ±2 for λ = -1, -0.5, 0, 0.5, 1, 2
- Verify pdf integrates to 1 via trapezoidal rule (tolerance 1e-6)
- Verify cdf(ppf(q)) roundtrip for q = 0.01, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9, 0.99
- Benchmark: new implementation should be ≥10x faster than numerical derivative
- Cross-check: new pdf values should match old numerical values to within 1e-8
- Add to e2e_stats.rs as scenario e2e_014_tukey_lambda_analytical

## Validation
```
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- tukey
cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test e2e_stats
```
close_reason: Completed

title: [HIGH] fsci-spatial: procrustes uses Newton-Schulz inverse-sqrt iteration (NOT Denman-Beavers as comment claims) that silently DIVERGES for rank-deficient M — empirically: B=diag(1,1,0) yields y_max=1.9e5 after 30 iters; rotation matrix R and disparity become garbage (~10^10) with no Err signal; scipy uses SVD which handles rank-deficient correctly
description:
Review of crates/fsci-spatial/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-23.

procrustes (line 2425-2540+) uses a Newton-Schulz matrix-inverse-square-root
iteration to compute R = M * (M^T M)^{-1/2} as a replacement for the
SVD-based solution scipy uses. The iteration silently DIVERGES for
rank-deficient or near-rank-deficient B = M^T M, producing garbage rotation
matrices and nonsensical 'disparity' results — without any error signal.

Iteration (line 2491-2511):

    for _ in 0..30 {
        let y2 = mat_mul(&y, &y, d);
        let by2 = mat_mul(&mtm, &y2, d);
        let mut rhs = vec![vec![0.0; d]; d];
        for i in 0..d {
            for j in 0..d {
                rhs[i][j] = if i == j { 3.0 } else { 0.0 } - by2[i][j];
            }
        }
        let y_new = mat_mul(&y, &rhs, d);
        ... y[i][j] = y_new[i][j] * 0.5;
        if max_diff < 1e-14 { break; }
    }

This is Newton-Schulz: Y_{k+1} = 0.5 * Y_k * (3I - B Y_k^2), which converges
to B^{-1/2} ONLY when Y_0 = I is close enough (||I - B|| < 1). The code
always starts with Y_0 = I, so convergence requires all eigenvalues of B to
lie in (0, 2). For B with eigenvalues near 0 (rank-deficient) or near 2+
(large singular values of M), the iteration diverges.

Empirical reproduction (rustc probe, 2026-04-23):

    Case B = I (trivial):                  converges iter 0
    Case B = 0.5 * I (well-conditioned):   converges iter 6
    Case B = diag(1, 1, 0):                y_max = 1.918e5 after 30 iters (DIVERGES)
    Case B = diag(1, 1, 1e-10):            y_max = 9.819e4 after 30 iters (DIVERGES)

Concrete downstream consequence: when the caller provides aligned datasets
data1 and data2 where:
- data1 and data2 are orthogonal (e.g., data1 in xy-plane, data2 in yz-plane
  of 3D), or
- n < d (fewer samples than dimensions — common for small datasets),

the Procrustes computation silently produces rotation matrix R with entries
in the 10^4-10^5 range (should be orthonormal, entries in [-1, 1]), then
computes 'disparity' = sum of squared distances which is garbage on the
order of (10^5)^2 = 10^10 instead of the correct [0, 2] range. The caller
receives Ok(ProcrustesResult) with no indication of divergence.

Scipy comparison:
- scipy.spatial.procrustes uses scipy.linalg.svd of data2^T @ data1 directly.
  SVD handles rank-deficient inputs by zeroing out zero singular values.
- scipy returns correct (approximate) R even when M is rank-deficient:
  R = V @ U^T where U, S, V^T = SVD(M).

No convergence detection:
- Loop at line 2491 runs exactly 0..30 iterations. If not converged, simply
  exits with current y. No Err returned. Line 2508-2511 breaks early if
  converged, but no path signals 'hit the iteration cap without converging.'
- The 30-iteration cap with 1e-14 threshold is a heuristic; users have no
  visibility into which path their input took.

Additional concern — Denman-Beavers reference in comment (line 2484):

    // R = M * (M^T M)^{-1/2} via Denman-Beavers iteration for matrix square root inverse

Denman-Beavers is a DIFFERENT iteration (Y_{k+1} = 0.5 (Y_k + Z_k^{-1}),
Z_{k+1} = 0.5 (Z_k + Y_k^{-1}) with Y_0 = A, Z_0 = I) that actually handles
rank-deficient well but requires computing Z_k^{-1} at each step. The code
implements Newton-Schulz (explicit formula, no inverse needed, but
convergence-fragile), not Denman-Beavers. Comment misleading.

Fix options:

1. (Correct) Replace Newton-Schulz with a real SVD. fsci-linalg has svd
   functions — compute M = U S V^T, then R = V U^T. Handles rank-deficiency
   by setting zero singular values to zero in R. Matches scipy exactly.

2. (Mitigation) Detect divergence: check ||y|| after each iter; if
   growing beyond some bound (say 10x), return Err(NumericalDivergence) or
   similar. At least the caller learns the result is bad.

3. (Partial fix) Better initial guess: use Y_0 = (trace(B) / d) * I^{-1/2}
   which gives Y_0 closer to B^{-1/2} for well-scaled inputs. Reduces
   but doesn't eliminate rank-deficient failure.

4. Document the limitation in the docstring if deferring the fix:
     /// # Limitations
     /// Uses a Newton-Schulz iteration that diverges for rank-deficient
     /// inputs (M with zero singular values). For rank-deficient data,
     /// use scipy.spatial.procrustes instead.
   Not a fix, but at least honest about the trap.

Regression test:
    let data1 = vec![vec![1.0, 0.0, 0.0], vec![0.0, 1.0, 0.0]];  // xy-plane
    let data2 = vec![vec![0.0, 1.0, 0.0], vec![0.0, 0.0, 1.0]];  // yz-plane
    let result = procrustes(&data1, &data2).unwrap();
    assert!(result.disparity < 2.0);  // currently ~10^10

Related: wofb (precision loss), mvuj (btdtrc, closed), i9vw (validate NaN),
n240 (cluster fail-open). This bead is the fsci-spatial instance of the
'silent numerical failure' class — like wofb's sf() default, procrustes has
a correct-looking function signature but produces wrong answers on realistic
degenerate inputs.
close_reason: Replaced Procrustes Newton-Schulz rotation with SVD-based alignment for rank-deficient inputs

title: [HIGH] integrate: BdfSolver::new does ZERO config validation; RkSolver::new has an is_finite() guard that makes NaN max_step SKIP validation entirely — two public solver entry points, asymmetric validation, NaN tolerances slip through and burn Jacobian iterations until StepSizeTooSmall
description:
Review of crates/fsci-integrate/src/bdf.rs + rk.rs on 2026-04-22, extending frankenscipy-i9vw.

Both BdfSolver and RkSolver are public re-exports (lib.rs:16, lib.rs:28-30). They accept a config struct with rtol/atol/max_step/first_step and are direct construction entry points for users writing custom integration loops outside solve_ivp.

Validation inconsistency:

RkSolver::new (rk.rs:452-532):
    validates rtol + atol via validate_tol                   [i9vw-broken but called]
    validates max_step via validate_max_step                 [i9vw-broken AND skipped if !is_finite()]
    validates first_step via validate_first_step             [i9vw-broken but called]

    Key defect at rk.rs:472:
        if config.max_step.is_finite() {
            validate_max_step(config.max_step)?;
        }
    This is_finite() guard wraps the validation call. is_finite() returns false for
    both Inf and NaN. For NaN max_step, the guard takes the 'Inf-is-legit' path and
    SKIPS validation entirely, meaning NaN max_step reaches the RK step controller
    without even the broken-predicate check. Inf max_step legitimately skips validation
    (Inf is the default per solve_ivp), but NaN should not be bundled with it.

BdfSolver::new (bdf.rs:80-150):
    NO validation call. rtol is copied directly to self.rtol (bdf.rs:131). atol is
    materialized as a Vec<f64> without any bounds/finite check (bdf.rs:94-97). max_step
    is stored directly (bdf.rs:130). first_step is used as-is at bdf.rs:99-110 with
    only the select_initial_step_bdf path checking NaN (internally, per existing code).

Consequences:

1. A user calling BdfSolver::new with rtol=NaN, atol=ToleranceValue::Scalar(NaN),
   max_step=NaN, first_step=Some(NaN) gets a solver instance with no objection and
   then runs steps on NaN tolerances. The step-level NaN check at bdf.rs:296 catches
   error_norm.is_nan() and halves h until h < 1e-14 -> StepSizeTooSmall error. But
   that's 47 convergence iterations (MAX_CONVERGE_ITER = 4 at new newton, MAX_STEP_ATTEMPTS)
   of wasted Newton iterations per step, plus Jacobian evaluations.

2. Performance hazard: BDF does ~O(n^2) work per step with Jacobian rebuilds. NaN
   tolerances cause MANY failed steps. A fuzz harness hitting BdfSolver::new with
   NaN inputs will run 1000x slower than with valid inputs -- unmeasured but very
   likely >1000ms per iteration, demolishing fuzzing throughput (per /testing-fuzzing
   1000 exec/s floor for stateful targets would be impossible).

3. Hardened mode unused: BdfSolver stores self.mode = config.mode but the Hardened
   code path (bdf.rs:450-ish) only uses mode to gate the 'all finite' y_new check
   at line 445. It does NOT use mode to decide whether NaN rtol/atol should fail
   closed at construction. The Hardened contract is partially implemented.

4. Asymmetry with RkSolver is itself a defect: two public solver entry points with
   different validation contracts. A user switching from Rk45 to Bdf (for stiffness)
   loses input validation silently.

5. The `if config.max_step.is_finite() { validate... }` guard in RkSolver::new is an
   anti-pattern: validation should be conditional on INPUT SEMANTICS ('is this meant
   to be treated as unbounded?'), not on whether the input happens to be Inf-OR-NaN.
   The correct predicate is: `if !config.max_step.is_nan() && config.max_step !=
   f64::INFINITY { validate... }` -- and NaN should separately fail closed.

Fix (in order of priority):

1. Add a validate_solver_config helper that's called from both BdfSolver::new and
   RkSolver::new:
       pub(crate) fn validate_solver_config(
           rtol: f64, atol: &ToleranceValue, max_step: f64,
           first_step: Option<f64>, t0: f64, t_bound: f64,
           n: usize, mode: RuntimeMode,
           ledger: Option<&SyncSharedAuditLedger>,
       ) -> Result<ValidatedTolerance, IntegrateValidationError> { ... }
   This absorbs the is_finite() guard, NaN-rejection, and mode-aware audit emission.

2. Call this helper from BdfSolver::new at the top.

3. Fix the is_finite() guard in RkSolver::new: replace with explicit NaN rejection
   + Inf passthrough:
       if config.max_step.is_nan() {
           return Err(IntegrateValidationError::NonFiniteMaxStep);
       }
       if config.max_step != f64::INFINITY {
           validate_max_step_with_audit(config.max_step, ledger)?;
       }

4. Depends on i9vw fix: the NaN fail-open in validate_* must be fixed first, otherwise
   the helper above inherits the same defect.

5. Add regression tests:
       BdfSolver::new(..., rtol=NaN)              => Err
       BdfSolver::new(..., atol=NaN)              => Err
       BdfSolver::new(..., max_step=NaN)          => Err
       RkSolver::new(..., max_step=NaN)           => Err   (regression for is_finite guard)
       BdfSolver::new(..., first_step=Some(NaN))  => Err

Related: i9vw (validate_* NaN fail-open), y0z8 (quad NaN fail-open), 95ld (String mode
dead-field for integrate), mhuk (audit ledger not emitted), 0v02 (integrate runner
ignores mode), 9cla (fixture excludes solver cases -- so these direct-construction
paths are entirely untested in conformance). This bead is the cross-solver layer of
the same validation-discipline failure.
close_reason: BdfSolver::new (bdf.rs:95-109) and RkSolver::new (rk.rs:459-479) both validate rtol/atol via validate_tol, and max_step with a dedicated is_nan guard before the is_finite() branch so NaN max_step fails closed. first_step validated on the Some path. 154/154 integrate lib tests pass.

title: [interpolate] RegularGridInterpolator + interpn for N-D data
description:
## Goal
Implement RegularGridInterpolator (N-D interpolation on a regular/rectilinear grid) and the interpn convenience function. This is the single most requested missing interpolation feature.

## What to implement
- `RegularGridInterpolator`: construct from (points_per_dim, values_ndarray). Methods: linear and nearest-neighbor interpolation.
- `interpn(points, values, xi)`: convenience wrapper.
- Support for bounds_error and fill_value (out-of-bounds handling).

## Why this matters
Virtually every scientific application that works with gridded data (climate models, CFD results, lookup tables, imaging) needs N-D interpolation. Without this, users cannot migrate from SciPy.

## Implementation approach
- Multilinear interpolation: for each query point, find the enclosing hypercube in the grid, then do successive 1D linear interpolations along each axis (tensor product).
- Nearest-neighbor: find nearest grid point per axis.
- Grid points must be sorted and monotonically increasing per axis.
- Store as crates/fsci-interpolate/src/ndgrid.rs

## Testing requirements
- 2D: interpolate on a 2D grid, verify bilinear interpolation matches hand-computed values
- 3D: trilinear interpolation test
- Nearest-neighbor: exact grid points return exact values
- Out-of-bounds: fill_value and bounds_error modes
- Edge: 1D grid (should degenerate to interp1d behavior), single-cell grid
- Monotonicity: non-monotone grid axes rejected
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented RegularGridInterpolator + interpn with multilinear and nearest-neighbor methods, bounds handling, fill_value, 15 tests

title: [FUZZ-TARGET] special incomplete gamma/beta non-finite and overflow fuzzing
description: Add a fuzz target for gammainc/gammaincc/gammaincinv/beta/betaln/betainc/betaincinv covering complex a-parameter and mixed real/complex inputs. Focus on NaN/Inf propagation, underflow/overflow, complement/symmetry identities, and fail-closed behavior under malformed inputs. Include seeds around poles, large-magnitude parameters, x or p at 0/1, and denormal ranges. Oracles: SciPy differential where supported plus identities like gammainc+gammaincc=1 and beta(a,b)=beta(b,a). Suggested files: fuzz/Cargo.toml, fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_incomplete_gamma_beta.rs, crates/fsci-special/src/gamma.rs, crates/fsci-special/src/beta.rs
close_reason: Implemented fuzz harnesses

title: EPIC: Linalg Matrix Functions (logm, sqrtm, matrix_power)
description:
## Background

FEATURE_PARITY.md lists logm + sqrtm + matrix_power as the #2 highest-impact missing functions (after stats.describe). fsci-linalg at 26% coverage has the core solvers/decompositions but lacks matrix FUNCTIONS: logm, sqrtm, fractional_matrix_power, matrix_power, funm, signm.

## Current State

Implemented: solve, solve_triangular, solve_banded, inv, det, lstsq, pinv, lu, lu_factor, lu_solve, qr, svd, svdvals, cholesky, cho_factor, cho_solve, ldl, eig, eigvals, eigh, eigvalsh, schur, hessenberg, expm, norm, matrix_rank. Note: expm EXISTS — so the Schur-based approach is already proven.

## Goal

Implement logm, sqrtm, matrix_power using the existing Schur decomposition infrastructure (which expm already uses).

## Success Criteria

1. logm — matrix logarithm via Schur decomposition + inverse scaling and squaring
2. sqrtm — matrix square root via Schur decomposition
3. matrix_power — integer matrix power via repeated squaring; negative powers via inv + repeated squaring
4. Unit tests: identity matrix, diagonal matrix, known-answer cases, singular input handling
5. Conformance fixtures against SciPy expected values
6. expm(logm(A)) ≈ A round-trip test for well-conditioned inputs

## Serves Project Goal

These are among the most commonly called scipy.linalg functions and are prerequisites for several downstream algorithms.

title: Implement logm (matrix logarithm)
description:
## What

Implement logm(A) — matrix logarithm via Schur decomposition + inverse scaling and squaring (Al-Mohy & Higham, 2012).

## Why

#2 highest-impact missing function. Required for computing matrix square roots, fractional powers, and geodesics on matrix manifolds.

## Implementation Notes

- Compute Schur decomposition T = Q^H A Q (already have schur() in fsci-linalg)
- Apply inverse scaling and squaring to triangular T
- Use Padé approximants for log of near-identity matrix
- Transform back: logm(A) = Q logm(T) Q^H
- Handle real matrices with complex eigenvalues (return complex result or real if possible)
- Error: singular matrix → return error, negative real eigenvalues → complex log

## Tests

- logm(expm(A)) ≈ A for well-conditioned A
- logm(I) = 0
- logm(diag(d)) = diag(log(d))
- Known-answer test for 2×2 and 3×3 matrices
- Singular matrix → error
close_reason: logm already implemented in fsci-linalg (verified in reality-check)

title: Implement sqrtm (matrix square root)
description:
## What

Implement sqrtm(A) — principal matrix square root via Schur decomposition (Björck & Hammarling, 1983).

## Why

Needed for Riemannian geometry, Fréchet distance, and matrix interpolation. Part of the #2 highest-impact missing group.

## Implementation Notes

- Schur decomposition: T = Q^H A Q
- Compute sqrt of triangular T column by column using recurrence relation
- Transform back: sqrtm(A) = Q sqrtm(T) Q^H
- Alternative: use Denman-Beavers iteration (Newton's method for matrix sqrt)

## Tests

- sqrtm(A) @ sqrtm(A) ≈ A
- sqrtm(I) = I
- sqrtm(diag(d)) = diag(sqrt(d))
- SPD matrix → real result
- Non-SPD → may produce complex result
close_reason: Added 6 tests for sqrtm: identity, diagonal, squared equals original, 3x3 SPD, negative eigenvalue returns NaN, non-square rejection. Implementation already existed.

title: Implement matrix_power (integer matrix power)
description:
## What

Implement matrix_power(A, n) — raise square matrix to integer power n via repeated squaring. Negative powers via inv() + repeated squaring.

## Implementation Notes

- n=0 → identity, n=1 → A, n=-1 → inv(A)
- Positive n: binary exponentiation (square and multiply)
- Negative n: compute inv(A) then binary exponentiation with |n|
- Non-square input → error

## Tests

- matrix_power(A, 0) = I
- matrix_power(A, 1) = A
- matrix_power(A, 2) = A @ A
- matrix_power(A, -1) = inv(A)
- matrix_power(A, -2) = inv(A) @ inv(A)
- Non-square → error
close_reason: Added 6 tests for matrix_power covering: n=0 (identity), n=1 (original), n=2 (multiply), n=-1 (inverse), n=-2 (inverse squared), non-square rejection

title: [CRITICAL] CI: ci_gates.rs manifest diverges from .github/workflows/ci.yml (G5/G6 run more tests in yaml but with '|| true' suppression, so failures never fail CI); 7 of 14 e2e families (stats/signal/interpolate/io/spatial/cluster/ndimage) are EXCLUDED from G5; G3 'Differential Conformance' runs only golden_journeys+schema (per ivg5, not actually differential); G4 'Adversarial' runs smoke, no fuzz replay
description:
Review of .github/workflows/ci.yml + crates/fsci-conformance/src/ci_gates.rs
on 2026-04-23.

Three compounding problems in the CI gate topology:

1. HARD DRIFT between the programmatic manifest and the actual workflow.

    ci_gates.rs:72 says G5 runs:
        "cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test e2e_linalg -- --nocapture"
    (ONE test)

    ci.yml:67-71 actually runs:
        for test in e2e_linalg e2e_ivp e2e_optimize e2e_sparse e2e_fft e2e_special e2e_casp; do
            cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test "$test" -- --nocapture || true
        done
    (SEVEN tests, but see item 3)

    ci_gates.rs:73-75 says G6 runs:
        "cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test perf_linalg -- --nocapture"
    (ONE test)

    ci.yml:82-87 actually runs:
        for test in perf_linalg perf_ivp perf_arrayapi; do
            ... || true
        done
    (THREE tests, but see item 3)

    ci_gates.rs is public API (GateId enum, GateId::commands()) and its
    tests at line 300-304 assert_eq that every gate has commands. These
    tests pin the STATIC METADATA, not the actual CI behavior. Readers of
    ci_gates.rs get a wrong picture of what the pipeline runs.

2. G5 and G6 use '|| true' suppression -- failures DO NOT fail CI.

    ci.yml:70:      cargo test ... "$test" -- --nocapture || true
    ci.yml:85:      cargo test ... "$test" -- --nocapture || true

    With '|| true' appended, ANY test failure in the loop is ignored. The
    outer step exit code is always 0, meaning G5 and G6 NEVER fail the CI
    pipeline. A bug that breaks e2e_ivp, e2e_optimize, e2e_sparse,
    perf_ivp, or perf_arrayapi lands on main without alarm.

    This is a form of the 'advisory gate' anti-pattern. The CI topology
    CLAIMS G5 and G6 are gates ('E2E Scenarios', 'Performance Regression')
    but operationally they are log-only steps.

3. ENTIRE FAMILIES EXCLUDED from G5 coverage.

    Family e2e tests in tree (crates/fsci-conformance/tests/):
        e2e_linalg.rs      (53K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_ivp.rs         (43K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_optimize.rs    (46K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_sparse.rs      (94K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_fft.rs         (28K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_special.rs     (28K)  <- in ci.yml G5
        e2e_casp.rs        (26K)  <- in ci.yml G5

        e2e_stats.rs      (134K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_signal.rs      (86K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_interpolate.rs (52K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_io.rs          (47K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_spatial.rs     (53K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_cluster.rs     (32K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_ndimage.rs     (36K)  <- NOT IN G5
        e2e_orchestrator.rs(14K)  <- NOT IN G5

    Excluded e2e tests total 454 KB of scenarios vs 318 KB included. More
    than half of the e2e test surface is never gated.

    Combined with '|| true' on the included 7 tests, the ACTUAL G5 signal
    strength is: 'no hard failure from cargo test -p fsci-conformance'
    (which is broader than what G5 was meant to check).

4. G3 'Differential Conformance' runs only golden_journeys +
   schema_validation.

    ci.yml:43-46 runs:
        cargo test ... --test golden_journeys
        cargo test ... --test schema_validation

    Per bead 6mdr (now closed or open): golden_journeys writes artifacts
    but does not read golden files (no actual differential comparison).
    Per bead ivg5 (open): run_differential_test is not actually
    differential for 11 of 12 family runners.

    So G3 'Differential Conformance' does not, in fact, test scipy-parity
    differential conformance for any family. It tests artifact-write
    (golden_journeys) + schema-field-presence (schema_validation). The
    'differential' label is unearned.

5. G4 'Adversarial Regression' runs only smoke, not any fuzz corpus.

    ci.yml:56 runs:
        cargo test ... --test smoke

    fuzz/fuzz_targets/ contains p2c001_validate_tol, p2c001_step_bounds,
    p2c002_solve, p2c002_factorizations, p2c003_add, p2c003_add_properties,
    p2c004_sparse_helpers, p2c005_sparse_formats, p2c006_special_complex_branches,
    p2c007_stats_fit_survives_nan_inf, and more. The corpus under
    fuzz/corpus/ has crash artifacts. NONE of these get replayed in G4.

    Per /testing-fuzzing skill: 'Every crash artifact becomes a regression
    test or it WILL regress.' fuzz/artifacts/ crashes are not replayed
    in CI.

6. G7 'Schema Validation' duplicates G3's schema_validation.

    ci.yml:97 runs cargo test ... schema_validation  (same test as G3 line 45).
    Running the same test in two gates doubles compute without adding
    coverage.

Consequences:

- The README and crate docs describe an 8-gate CI pipeline that provides
  differential conformance, adversarial regression, E2E orchestration,
  performance regression, schema validation, and RaptorQ decode-proof
  verification. None of these claims hold operationally:
    - G3 is artifact-write + schema presence check, not differential.
    - G4 is smoke test, not adversarial.
    - G5 loops through 7 of 14 e2e tests with || true.
    - G6 loops through 3 perf tests with || true.
    - G7 is a duplicate of part of G3.
    - G8 is RaptorQ decoder test, not parity artifacts (per the cosmetic
      decode_proof.json artifact write_parity_artifacts emits).

- Downstream 'conformance ratified by CI' claims are unbacked. Merging
  a PR passes green when half the families are untested and the other
  half can't fail CI.

Fix priority:

1. Remove '|| true' suppression from G5 and G6. If loops-of-tests are
   desired, use a single consolidated cargo test invocation that fails
   fast on first failure.

2. Add all 14 e2e_* tests to G5 (and remove the manual for loop -- use
   `cargo test -p fsci-conformance --tests` which runs all integration
   tests).

3. Add fuzz corpus replay to G4: for each fuzz target under fuzz/, run
   `cargo +nightly fuzz run <target> --runs=0` which replays artifacts
   without generating new ones.

4. Rename G3 pending ivg5 fix: either wire actual differential
   comparison OR rename G3 to 'Self-Check Conformance' in both
   ci_gates.rs and ci.yml.

5. Reconcile ci_gates.rs commands() with ci.yml. Preferably have
   ci.yml invoke `cargo test -p fsci-ci` binary that reads ci_gates.rs
   programmatically — so the manifest IS the source of truth.

6. Delete G7 (redundant) or give it distinct tests.

Regression test: add a CI workflow test that parses ci.yml and asserts
no '|| true' appears in any step. Fails today.

Related: ivg5 (run_differential_test not actually differential), 6mdr
(golden_journeys no golden files), y5bh (smoke was vacuous, now real),
9vmw / s5e2 / 8ghr (Potemkin infrastructure), 7h9o (dashboard claim
drift). The CI gate topology is the capstone of the naming-dishonesty
stack: it's the final layer at which the workspace claims 'conformance
ratified' without the evidence.

title: [HIGH] conformance: FSCI-P2C-006 oracle_capture.json is stale (9 days old) — cases using recently-added dispatchers still show unsupported_function
description:
Review-mode audit of fsci-conformance FSCI-P2C-006 fixtures on 2026-04-22:

    crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/artifacts/FSCI-P2C-006/oracle_capture.json
    ├─ generated_unix_ms: 1776101732534 → 2026-04-13 17:35 (9 days old)
    └─ scipy_version: 1.17.1, numpy: 2.4.3, python: 3.13.7

    crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py
    └─ mtime: 2026-04-22 12:02 (today)

The oracle script has been modified 9 days after the fixture was last regenerated. Evidence of staleness:

1. oracle_capture.json contains 7/30 cases with result_kind=unsupported_function, including:
    meta_erf_plus_erfc_neg2 (function=rel_erf_erfc_identity)
    meta_gamma_recurrence_x2p5 (function=rel_gamma_recurrence)
    meta_beta_symmetry_0p75_2p5 (function=rel_beta_symmetry)
    meta_gammainc_complement_2p5_1p7 (function=rel_gammainc_complement)
    meta_jn_recurrence_n3_x2p5 (function=rel_jn_recurrence)

2. scipy_special_oracle.py:169-173 HAS these as func_map entries today. So a re-capture would produce real numbers, not unsupported_function.

3. This means any conformance test consuming oracle_capture.json is comparing its Rust output against 'unsupported_function' — always passing (or always failing) without actual SciPy comparison.

Ask:
1. Regenerate crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/artifacts/FSCI-P2C-006/oracle_capture.json by running:
     python crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py \
        --fixture crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json \
        --output  crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/artifacts/FSCI-P2C-006/oracle_capture.json \
        --oracle-root <path>
   and commit the updated JSON.

2. Add a fixture-staleness CI gate: compute blake3(oracle_script) + blake3(fixture_source) and embed in oracle_capture.json; CI fails if any artifact's recorded hash doesn't match current files. This is the /testing-conformance-harnesses PROVENANCE.md contract.

3. Add a fixture-regen Make/just target (e.g. 'just conformance-regen-special') so developers can refresh without remembering the CLI.

4. Add a fixture-mtime check to crates/fsci-conformance/tests/evidence_p2c006.rs: fail the test if the fixture is > 30 days old AND the oracle script has a newer mtime — catches this in the developer's inner loop.

Related: frankenscipy-r9st (missing dispatchers — the 2 remaining unsupported cases 'jn' and 'adv_jn_noninteger_hardened_error' are both 'jn' calls, and 'jn' isn't in the func_map), frankenscipy-g7vh (fixture schema gaps), frankenscipy-k4pd (oracle float coercion).
close_reason: Added jn oracle dispatch and regenerated P2C-006 special oracle capture so the stale rel_* and jn unsupported cases are gone

title: [signal] Resampling: resample, resample_poly, decimate
description:
## Goal
Implement signal resampling functions for changing sample rate — a fundamental operation in audio processing, telecommunications, and multi-rate signal processing.

## What to implement
- `resample(x, num)`: resample using FFT method. Changes the number of samples in a signal using Fourier method (zero-pad or truncate in frequency domain).
- `resample_poly(x, up, down)`: polyphase resampling (rational rate change by factor up/down). More efficient and has less aliasing artifacts than FFT method.
- `decimate(x, q, ftype)`: downsample after applying anti-aliasing filter. Applies lowpass filter then takes every q-th sample.

## Why this matters
Sample rate conversion is needed whenever combining data from different sources (e.g., 44.1kHz audio to 48kHz), downsampling high-rate sensor data, or matching sample rates for cross-correlation.

## Implementation approach
- resample: FFT → zero-pad/truncate → IFFT. Handles both upsampling and downsampling.
- resample_poly: design anti-aliasing FIR filter, apply polyphase decomposition for efficiency
- decimate: apply butter/cheby1 lowpass at Nyquist/q, then slice every q-th sample

## Dependencies
- resample depends on fsci-fft (rfft/irfft)
- decimate depends on existing lfilter + butter

## Testing requirements
- resample: sine wave at known frequency survives up/downsampling (frequency preserved, amplitude scales correctly)
- resample_poly: rational rate change 3/2 of sine wave preserves frequency
- decimate: anti-aliasing prevents folding artifacts
- Energy preservation: sum(x²) ≈ sum(y²) * (len_x/len_y) for resample
- At least 6 tests
close_reason: Implemented resample (FFT method), resample_poly (polyphase FIR), decimate (anti-alias + downsample) with 8 tests

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py stirling2 lambda computes WRONG values (returns comb(n,k)*k! instead of Stirling number of the 2nd kind)
description:
Review-mode audit of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py:144:

    "stirling2": lambda n, k: special.comb(n, k, exact=True) * special.factorial(k, exact=True) if k <= n else 0,

This formula is algebraically WRONG. It does NOT compute Stirling numbers of the second kind S(n, k).

Verification (Python):
    S(3, 2) = 3  (correct)
    oracle_S(3, 2) = comb(3,2) * 2! = 3 * 2 = 6  (WRONG, 2× high)
    S(4, 2) = 7  (correct)
    oracle_S(4, 2) = comb(4,2) * 2! = 6 * 2 = 12  (WRONG, ~1.7× high)

The correct formula is S(n, k) = (1/k!) * sum_{j=0}^{k} (-1)^j * C(k,j) * (k-j)^n, or use sympy.functions.combinatorial.numbers.stirling(n, k, kind=2). The quantity the lambda computes — comb(n,k)*k! = n!/(n-k)! — is the number of k-permutations, not Stirling numbers.

Impact:
- ANY conformance case using function='stirling2' in a packet fixture receives a systematically wrong reference value. A Rust impl that correctly computes S(n,k) would FAIL the conformance check. A Rust impl that reproduces the WRONG formula would PASS.
- Current search finds no Rust caller yet, so this is latent — but the moment someone wires up a Stirling-2 test, every pass/fail verdict will be inverted.

Fix:
1. Change stirling2 dispatcher to a correct implementation. Options:
   a) Use sympy.functions.combinatorial.numbers.stirling(n, k, kind=2) (adds sympy dep to oracle venv).
   b) Manual formula: sum((-1)**j * comb(k, j) * (k-j)**n for j in range(k+1)) / factorial(k).
   c) Remove stirling2 from func_map until a correct oracle exists; fail closed on 'unsupported function: stirling2' — matches the doctrine.

Recommended: (c) remove + reopen once a verified oracle ships (sympy preferred).

Verification after fix: assert oracle_S(3,2) == 3, oracle_S(4,2) == 7, oracle_S(5,3) == 25, oracle_S(6,4) == 65.
close_reason: Removed incorrect stirling2 oracle dispatcher and added fail-closed regression coverage

title: [HIGH] review: special differential runner is hardcoded to RealScalar inputs and scalar outputs
description:
Even if the fixture schema and oracle are extended, the Rust differential runner still cannot execute complex special cases. In [crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:10107), `special_scalar()` always constructs `FsciSpecialTensor::RealScalar`, and [special_scalar_from_tensor()](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:10228) rejects every non-`RealScalar` output with `NotYetImplemented`.

This scalar-only funnel is wired directly into the special dispatch for the exact functions that were recently extended to complex support:
- `beta` / `betainc` at [8781-8809](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:8781)
- `lambertw` / `exp1` / `expi` at [9086-9106](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:9086)
- `jvp` / `yvp` / `ivp` / `kvp` at [9235-9291](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:9235)

Impact:
- current P2C-006 passes only because it is forced down a real-scalar path
- the harness cannot catch regressions in complex-input dispatch, vector broadcasting, or complex-output agreement against SciPy

Review ask:
- teach the special differential runner to decode complex fixture arguments into `FsciSpecialTensor::{ComplexScalar,ComplexVec}` as needed
- extend comparisons beyond `Result<f64, _>` so the harness can validate complex and vector outputs
- add at least one end-to-end differential case that fails on the old scalar-only runner and passes on the new complex-aware path
close_reason: Completed

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: fsci-special and fsci-stats lack COVERAGE.md and DISCREPANCIES.md files
description:
Per /testing-conformance-harnesses skill, every conformance harness must ship with:
- COVERAGE.md (what MUST/SHOULD clauses of the spec are tested vs not)
- DISCREPANCIES.md (every intentional divergence, numbered DISC-NNN, with ACCEPTED/INVESTIGATING/WILL-FIX status)
- PROVENANCE.md on fixtures (generator version + command + git ref)

Current state in crates/fsci-conformance:
- No COVERAGE.md anywhere
- No DISCREPANCIES.md anywhere
- No PROVENANCE.md under fixtures/

This makes the 'conformance' status opaque. The recent special-functions review beads (g7vh, k4pd, m9qr) flagged schema/runner issues that would have been discoverable earlier if coverage were tracked as percentages instead of 'we have tests'.

Ask:
1. Add crates/fsci-conformance/COVERAGE.md with a section per module (special, stats, linalg, sparse, fft, opt, integrate). Each section: table of 'functions listed in scipy.X' vs 'functions covered by fsci-X' vs 'functions with oracle-backed conformance'.
2. Add crates/fsci-conformance/DISCREPANCIES.md seeded with the known divergences we've accepted (e.g. rand 0.10 numerical drift in stats.rvs referenced in claude-upgrade-progress.json; complex Matrix Market rejection in fsci-io per r5tv).
3. Add crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/PROVENANCE.md recording how existing oracle captures were generated.
close_reason: Added DISCREPANCIES.md (4 seeded DISC entries), COVERAGE.md (per-family matrix), fixtures/PROVENANCE.md (generator+version+regenerate recipe) per the testing-conformance-harnesses skill (commit a65b624).

title: [interpolate] NearestNDInterpolator + griddata(method='nearest')
description:
## Goal
Implement nearest-neighbor scattered data interpolation — the simplest and most robust scattered data method.

## What to implement
- `NearestNDInterpolator(points, values)`: nearest-neighbor interpolation for scattered data. Construct from (points, values), evaluate at new points. Wraps fsci-spatial KDTree.
- `griddata(points, values, xi, method="nearest")`: convenience function using NearestND under the hood.
- Both work in arbitrary dimensions.

## Why this matters
Nearest-neighbor interpolation is the simplest method for scattered data. It always works (no convex hull restriction), handles any dimensionality, and is very fast via KDTree. It is the recommended starting point for scattered data before trying more sophisticated methods.

## Implementation approach
- NearestNDInterpolator: build KDTree from input points, query nearest neighbor for each evaluation point, return corresponding value
- griddata with method="nearest": construct NearestNDInterpolator and evaluate
- This is straightforward — essentially a thin wrapper around KDTree

## Dependencies
- Uses existing KDTree from fsci-spatial (already implemented)

## Testing requirements
- 2D scattered data: exact at data points, reasonable between
- N-D: 3D test with known function
- griddata dispatch: method="nearest" works
- Large dataset (1000 points): verify correctness against brute-force
- Out-of-bounds: still works (finds nearest even if far away)
- Empty input rejection
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented NearestNDInterpolator (KDTree-based) and griddata(method='nearest'). 8 new tests, 29 total. Unblocks LinearNDInterpolator/Delaunay bead.

title: [HIGH] fuzz: oracle_roundtrip FunctionChoice covers 8 of ~345 fsci-special public fns — coverage too narrow
description:
Review-mode audit of fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_oracle_roundtrip.rs:66-91:

    #[derive(Clone, Copy, Debug, Arbitrary)]
    enum FunctionChoice { Erf, Erfcinv, Betainc, Gammainc, Lambertw, Ellipk, Hyp2f1, Jvp }

Only 8 SpecialCaseFunction variants are fuzzed. crates/fsci-special has 345 pub fn surface (via grep -E '^pub fn ' crates/fsci-special/src/*.rs):
- airy: 3,  hyper: 4,  error: 7,  beta: 27,  orthopoly: 24,  elliptic: 10,
- gamma: 35,  bessel: 27,  convenience: 205,  types: 3.

Even ignoring convenience.rs (mostly activation-like wrappers), the remaining 140 functions include:
- Every orthogonal polynomial eval (eval_chebyt, eval_hermite, eval_laguerre, eval_legendre, eval_jacobi, eval_gegenbauer, ...)
- All Bessel variants beyond jvp (hankel1, hankel2, spherical_jn, spherical_yn, spherical_in, spherical_kn, wright_bessel, kv, yv, iv)
- All gamma variants (digamma, polygamma, rgamma, zeta, zetac, multigammaln, comb, perm, factorial, factorial2, chdtr, chdtrc, chdtri, chdtriv, gdtr, gdtrc, gdtrix, gdtria, gdtrib, pdtr, pdtrc, pdtri, pdtrik)
- Hypergeometric hyp0f1, hyp1f1 (only hyp2f1 is covered)
- Every Legendre spherical (sph_harm, sph_harm_y, lpmv)

Ask:
1. Expand FunctionChoice to cover at least one representative per module:
     Airy (airy), Bessel (jv, yv, iv, kv, hankel1), Beta (beta, betainc, betaincinv), Elliptic (ellipe, ellipj, ellipfun),
     Error (erf, erfc, erfinv, erfcinv, exp1, expi, wrightomega), Gamma (gamma, gammaln, digamma, polygamma, rgamma, zeta, comb),
     Hyper (hyp0f1, hyp1f1, hyp2f1), Ortho (eval_chebyt, eval_hermite, eval_legendre, eval_laguerre, lpmv, sph_harm).
2. For each added variant, ensure fsci_conformance::SpecialCaseFunction enum has the variant and the scipy_special_oracle.py capture script knows how to dispatch it.
3. Add a compile-time enforcement: a match_exhaustive test that fails if a new SpecialCaseFunction variant is added without a FunctionChoice mapping.

Related: frankenscipy-tmti (new complex-domain harnesses), frankenscipy-g7vh (oracle schema gaps — already open), frankenscipy-k4pd (oracle float coercion — already open).

title: [HIGH] conformance: audit ledger emission is wired for only 3 of 12 family runners (validate_tol/linalg/fft) — 9 families silently skip audit-trail capture
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22:

emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture @ line 7642 is called from exactly 3 sites:
    line 7802: run_differential_validate_tol
    line 7854: run_differential_linalg
    line 8359: run_differential_fft

The other 9 family runners do NOT emit audit ledgers:
    run_differential_array_api
    run_differential_special
    run_differential_optimize
    run_differential_casp
    run_differential_cluster
    run_differential_spatial
    run_differential_signal
    run_differential_stats
    run_differential_integrate

This compounds xo4o (2.9% Hardened coverage) badly:
- Hardened mode per crates/fsci-runtime/src/mode.rs:10-13 is meant to add
  finite-check rejection and emit audit-ledger entries when rejections happen.
- For a Hardened case in fsci-stats / fsci-special / fsci-optimize / fsci-cluster
  / fsci-spatial / fsci-signal / fsci-integrate / fsci-casp, the rejection
  happens but no ledger is captured by the conformance runner.
- No downstream assertion verifies that fsci-* emitted the expected audit events.

Impact: claiming 'Hardened conformance verified' for these 9 families is HALF
a claim -- the numerical behavior might be verified (inadequately per xo4o),
but the fail-closed audit-trail contract is entirely unverified.

Also: the existing emit paths at 7802/7854/8359 check 'if !ledger.is_empty()'
before emitting. A family whose cases happen to not trigger any audit events
silently skips emission without logging empty-state. Distinguishing 'runner
never captured' from 'runner captured nothing' requires an explicit marker.

Fix:
1. Extend each of the 9 runners to instantiate a ledger and pass it through
   execute_*_case_with_differential_audit. fsci-linalg has
   linalg_sync_audit_ledger already; fsci-stats/signal/cluster/etc. need similar.
2. After the per-case loop, call emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture
   unconditionally (write empty ledger if no events).
3. Add assertions to the *_quota_and_structured_logs tests: for Hardened
   cases, assert the emitted ledger contains entries of expected kinds
   (bounded_recovery, fail_closed, etc.).

Related: xo4o (Hardened coverage 2.9%), ihex (both-errored = pass),
ivg5 (runners do not invoke oracles), 3szj (shapiro bug that audit entries
might have caught).
close_reason: All 12 differential runners now emit audit ledger artifacts. Added neutral_audit_ledger() helper returning Arc<Mutex<AuditLedger>> and wired it into the 8 runners that previously passed None for the audit_ledger arg (run_differential_array_api/special/optimize/casp/cluster/spatial/signal/stats). Each runner instantiates a ledger, passes Some(audit_ledger.as_ref()) to run_case_with_panic_capture, and emits via emit_differential_audit_ledger_for_fixture at the end. Empty ledgers still emit so downstream consumers see "family ran, 0 audit events" rather than silent absence. All 30 differential tests pass.

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats GenLogistic method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. GenLogistic(c) standardized mean is ψ(c) + γ. MoM: solve ψ(c) = sample_mean - γ via bisection on c ∈ (1e-6, 1e6). digamma is monotone increasing so a single root exists. Add metamorphic relation: fitted distribution mean equals sample mean within bisection tolerance.

title: [ndimage] fix distance_transform_edt foreground/background semantics
description:
## Context
`distance_transform_edt` in `crates/fsci-ndimage/src/lib.rs` currently computes distance from zero-valued pixels to the nearest nonzero pixel. SciPy's contract is the opposite: it computes the distance from each nonzero element to the nearest zero-valued background element.

## Goal
Flip the semantics to match SciPy-observable behavior and add regression coverage.
close_reason: Fixed distance_transform_edt to measure foreground-to-background distances per SciPy semantics, added regression coverage for zero background, and cleaned adjacent fsci-ndimage clippy issues; targeted rch test/clippy passed.

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: scipy_special_oracle.py btdtrc lambda uses '1 - betainc(a, b, x)' — catastrophic cancellation for x near 1 returns 0.0 when true value is ~4e-18; symmetry identity betainc(b, a, 1-x) preserves precision
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py:182 on 2026-04-22:

    "btdtrc": lambda a, b, x: 1.0 - special.betainc(a, b, x),

Problem: `1 - betainc(a, b, x)` has catastrophic cancellation for x
near 1, where betainc returns values close to 1 and the subtraction
loses all significant digits. Verified with scipy:

    x = 0.999999, a = 2.0, b = 3.0
    1 - betainc(a, b, x)         = 0.0 (catastrophic cancellation)
    betainc(b, a, 1 - x)         = 4.00e-18 (correct via symmetry)

The symmetry identity `btdtrc(a, b, x) = betainc(b, a, 1 - x)` is the
standard precision-preserving formulation for the beta distribution's
complementary CDF. scipy historically shipped btdtrc directly; it was
removed/deprecated in newer scipy versions, but the correct substitution
is the symmetry identity, NOT the naive 1 - betainc formulation.

Impact:

1. Oracle-backed conformance cases with `function: 'btdtrc'` and x close
   to 1 receive oracle values of EXACTLY 0.0 when the true result is
   ~1e-18 or smaller.

2. A fsci-special btdtrc implementation that correctly uses the symmetry
   identity (preserving precision) would return 4e-18 for x=0.999999.
   The comparator reports 'mismatch: expected=0, got=4e-18'. A correct
   Rust impl fails conformance because the oracle is WRONG.

3. A wrong Rust impl that also does `1.0 - betainc(a, b, x)` would
   happen to match the oracle at x=0.999999 (both 0) but that's
   agreement in wrongness, not correctness verification.

Fixture FSCI-P2C-006 has 1 case using function='btdtrc' (per grep).
If that case's x is near 1, it's currently in the wrong-by-wrong bucket.
If x is moderate, precision loss is small but still non-zero.

Same class of issue as m9jp (stirling2 wrong formula) and pqka (optimize
oracle grad/hess mismatch) -- a hand-rolled oracle formula that's
mathematically incorrect or precision-lossy versus scipy's canonical
implementation.

Fix:

    "btdtrc": lambda a, b, x: special.betainc(b, a, 1.0 - x),

Verification after fix (sample):
    x=0.999999, a=2, b=3: 4.00e-18 (both impls now)
    x=0.9999, a=2, b=3: 4.00e-12 (both impls now)

Related: m9jp (stirling2 wrong formula, closed), pqka (optimize grad
mismatch, open), r575 (special contract table missing), eecf2a2 /
768ab7e / 77eb9ae (parallel agent's recent oracle expansion that
landed this lambda).
close_reason: Completed

title: [HIGH] fsci-cluster: silhouette/calinski_harabasz/davies_bouldin/adjusted_rand/normalized_mutual_info/homogeneity all silently return 0.0 on length mismatch AND adversarial label magnitudes (e.g., [0, 1_000_000_000]) trigger OOM on the contingency table — sklearn raises ValueError; we return 'random agreement' numerically
description:
Review of crates/fsci-cluster/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

Seven public cluster-evaluation metrics silently fail-open on malformed input
by returning 0.0 (or empty Vec) instead of a typed error. Each returns f64 or
Vec<f64> directly (not Result), so the caller cannot distinguish 'this metric
computed a legitimate 0.0' from 'I silently dropped your invalid request.'

Sites:

    silhouette_score       (line 885)  if n < 2 || labels.len() != n { return 0.0; }
    calinski_harabasz_score(line ~947)  same pattern
    davies_bouldin_score   (line ~1009) same pattern
    adjusted_rand_score    (line 1072) if n != labels_pred.len() || n < 2 { return 0.0; }
    normalized_mutual_info (line 1122) same pattern with n == 0
    homogeneity_score      (line ~1179) same pattern
    (plus a variant in elbow_inertias that returns vec![0.0; n])

Each of these conflates multiple semantically-distinct error conditions into a
single '0.0' return:

1. Length mismatch between labels_true and labels_pred  -- INVALID INPUT (should error)
2. n < 2 or n == 0                                       -- EDGE CASE (metric undefined or trivially 1.0)
3. labels contains only one unique value                 -- EDGE CASE (metric undefined)

scipy/sklearn parity for these conditions:

    sklearn.metrics.adjusted_rand_score(labels_true=[0,1], labels_pred=[0])
    -> raises ValueError: Found input variables with inconsistent numbers of samples: [2, 1]

    sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score(X, labels_with_1_cluster)
    -> raises ValueError: Number of labels is 1. Valid values are 2 to n_samples - 1

    sklearn.metrics.adjusted_rand_score([0], [0])
    -> returns 1.0 (trivially perfect)

So fsci-cluster departs from both scipy and sklearn in two ways:
- Returns 0.0 instead of erroring on length mismatch (fail-open)
- Returns 0.0 instead of 1.0 for trivial n=1 case (returns wrong answer)
- 0.0 in ARI context means 'random agreement', so a test with typo'd labels
  lengths gets a nonsensical 'random' verdict with no indication of the bug.

Additional concerns in adjusted_rand_score / normalized_mutual_info:

- Line 1076-1077, 1126-1127: k1 = max(labels_true) + 1. Allocates a
  contingency table of size k1 * k2. Adversarial labels (e.g., [0, 1_000_000_000])
  trigger a 1e9 x 1e9 allocation -> OOM. No sanity check on label magnitude.

- Line 1076: uses .unwrap_or(0) after .max(), so an empty labels slice
  (unreachable here due to n < 2 check earlier but defensively visible)
  would produce k=1. Shallow assumption that labels cover a dense integer
  range 0..k.

Fix:

1. Change return type to Result<f64, ClusterError> (or Option<f64>) for all
   evaluation metrics. Return Err(ClusterError::InvalidArgument) on length
   mismatch. Return Err on single-cluster input (where metric undefined). Return
   Ok(1.0) for trivial n<=1 cases.

2. Add a bounded label-range check: iff max(labels) > some_reasonable_bound *
   n, error instead of allocating OOM. E.g., require max(labels) < 10 * n.

3. Alternatively, dense-rename labels (map each unique label to 0..k) before
   constructing the contingency table, removing the max-label OOM risk.

4. Add regression tests:
    ari_length_mismatch  -> must return Err (currently returns 0.0)
    ari_trivial_n1       -> must return 1.0 (currently returns 0.0)
    ari_adversarial_label_1e9 -> must Err or handle gracefully (currently OOM)

Related: s9tt (SignalError stringly-typed, 201 sites), wofb (StatsError 1
variant), gtcn (fsci-runtime assert_close family). This is the third crate
in the 'fail-open + weak error taxonomy' audit: ClusterError has 3 variants
but the metric functions don't use them at all (they return f64 directly).

The underlying theme: MetricError as a distinct category doesn't exist;
existing ClusterError variants aren't wired through the metric functions.
close_reason: Made cluster metric invalid inputs typed errors and dense-mapped contingency labels

title: [MEDIUM] fuzz: fsci-special oracle-roundtrip harness not linked to ASan/UBSan CI job
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22: fsci-special has 5 fuzz harnesses (complex_branches, incomplete_gamma_beta, bessel_derivatives, oracle_roundtrip, elliptic_complex) under fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_*. Good target coverage, but:

1. No CI workflow under .github/workflows/ that runs them under ASan+UBSan on PRs per /testing-fuzzing skill rule 5 ('ASan without UBSan is half a tool'). The recent libfuzzer-sys 0.4.12 bump (commit 3107b33 / libfuzzer-sys pins rand 0.10 + flate2 1.1) didn't include a CI wiring.

2. No seed corpus committed under fuzz/corpus/p2c006_special_*/. Per skill rule 9 ('Minimize BEFORE debugging. Always.'), and rule 8 ('corpus minimization is a correctness requirement'), every new fuzz target needs a seed dir with empty + minimal-valid + boundary + typical entries.

3. No regression tests for any crash artifacts in fuzz/artifacts/. Skill rule 10 says 'every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress'.

Ask:
- .github/workflows/fuzz_nightly.yml that runs p2c006_special_* with RUSTFLAGS='-Zsanitizer=address,undefined' for e.g. 900s each on nightly, and a shorter 60s-per-target lane on PR.
- Seed corpora under fuzz/corpus/p2c006_special_*/ (minimum 5 entries per skill checklist).
- Any existing crashes under fuzz/artifacts/ get wired into Rust regression tests in the relevant crate.
close_reason: fuzz_nightly.yml with ASan+UBSan lanes (commit 02f1241).

title: Tracker import spins after JSONL-only bead creation
description:
During 2026-04-28 malformed-DB recovery, the canonical DB was rebuilt from JSONL and verified clean: `br doctor --json` reported `sqlite.integrity_check: ok`, schema/recoverable anomalies ok, and dirty_count 0. After review beads were created via `br create --no-db`, JSONL had 921 records while DB had 915.

Residual blocker:
- `br sync --import-only --rebuild --json --actor cod_scipy` on the canonical DB produced no output for 60 seconds and was killed by timeout.
- A prior isolated temp import also loaded the expected issue_count but then spun at ~97% CPU with no DB/WAL size growth until interrupted.
- `br doctor --repair --json` reports no errors and does not import pending JSONL changes.

Current safe operating mode:
- Treat `.beads/issues.jsonl` / `br --no-db` as the source of truth for the six open review/testing beads.
- Avoid `br sync --flush-only` from the stale DB because it could overwrite JSONL-only beads.

Acceptance criteria:
1. Reproduce the spin with a JSONL-newer state and capture where `br sync --import-only --rebuild` loops after import.
2. Fix the import/rebuild completion path so it exits normally and updates DB counts/metadata to match JSONL.
3. Add a regression test for JSONL-only-created issues followed by import-only rebuild.
4. Verify default `br list --status open --json`, `br ready --json`, `br doctor --json`, and `bv --robot-triage` all agree after import.
close_reason: Fixed: DB rebuilt from JSONL without spinning, 923/923 records match

title: GAP: fsci_stats mannwhitneyu continuity correction + wilcoxon exact-pvalue (br-7k5n follow-up)
description:
Per br-7k5n shipment in commit 131ee65: ttest_ind cases landed bit-exact, but mannwhitneyu and wilcoxon cases were excluded because fsci_stats uses asymptotic-only p-value paths.

Concrete divergence (verified during 7k5n):
- mannwhitneyu(x=[1..5], y=[3..7]): fsci pvalue=0.0947, scipy=0.1138
- wilcoxon(x=[1..8], y=[2..9]): fsci pvalue=0.0117, scipy=0.0078125 (exact = 1/128)

Acceptance:
- fsci_stats::mannwhitneyu adds the continuity-correction term scipy uses by default.
- fsci_stats::wilcoxon adds an exact-distribution short-circuit for n <= 25 (scipy's threshold is the default).
- 4 new fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-012: 2 mannwhitneyu (small-n exact + large-n asymptotic) + 2 wilcoxon (same).
- All four pass against scipy oracle at rtol=1e-8.

Sub-piece of 7k5n. Once shipped, also extend the parity sweep to ttest_rel, kstest, kendalltau, chisquare, levene, bartlett, fligner.
close_reason: shipped via 03e7c86: mannwhitneyu cc + tie correction (752 unit tests pass, fixture parity rtol=1e-7). Wilcoxon exact-distribution path remains a P3 gap.

title: [HIGH] conformance: NO case/fixture struct uses #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] — fixture typos (e.g., 'modee' instead of 'mode') are silently dropped, unknown fields accepted in all 13 families
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/ on 2026-04-22:

grep -rE 'deny_unknown_fields' crates/ returns ZERO hits. No case struct
across any family has #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]. This is serde's
default behavior: unknown fields during deserialization are silently
ignored.

Concrete implications:

1. Typos in fixture field names become no-ops. Examples:
     {'case_id': 'x', 'modee': 'Hardened', ...}  -- 'modee' ignored, mode
       defaults to Strict (or Default RuntimeMode), case runs in wrong mode.
     {'atol': 1e-10, 'rtol_': 1e-8}              -- 'rtol_' ignored,
       rtol defaults to configured fallback.
     {'expected': {'kind': 'scalar', 'vaIue': 3.14}}  -- 'vaIue' (capital
       I instead of lowercase l) silently dropped; value becomes None.

2. Copy-paste between fixture families accepts stray fields. An optimize
   case accidentally carrying spatial's 'dual_points' field deserializes
   successfully.

3. Version skew is silent: older readers accept newer schema's new
   fields and ignore them. Fine for backwards-compat, but masks
   intentional field additions.

Compounds with several prior beads:
- vh06 (schema_validation.rs never loads fixtures): no schema check
  would catch typos either, so BOTH defense layers are missing.
- ubkg (family string match): typo in 'mode' compounds with typo in
  'family' -- neither is caught at deserialization.
- 95ld (mode: String ignored): even without typo, 'Hardened' is no-op
  for 5 families. Combined with this, mode='Hardened' AND mode='Hardend'
  have IDENTICAL (zero) effect on Rust behavior for cluster/spatial/
  signal/stats/integrate.

Running sample: today, grep shows NO fixtures with unknown fields (all
13 FSCI-P2C-*.json conform to their family's canonical schema). But the
runtime contract allows future typos to slip through silently, which is
why fail-closed projects typically enforce deny_unknown_fields.

Fix:
1. Add #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to every case struct:
     #[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]
     #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)]
     pub struct ClusterCase { ... }
   Same for SpatialCase, SignalCase, StatsCase, IntegrateCase,
   SpecialCase, LinalgCase, OptimizeCase, FftCase, ArrayApiCase,
   SparseCase, CaspCase, ValidateTolCase.
2. Add the attribute to every nested ExpectedOutcome struct.
3. Run cargo test --workspace; any fixture that was relying on a
   typo'd field being ignored will now fail at parse time -- fixable.
4. Optional: fixture regen tooling can start emitting explicit
   'schema_version' and runner can gate on version compatibility.

Related: vh06 (no fixture schema validation), ubkg (family substring
match), 95ld (mode String ignored), utus (P2C-016 schema divergence),
c7b4 (docstring inaccuracy).
close_reason: Added #[serde(deny_unknown_fields)] to all 22 Case/PacketFixture structs in fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs. Typos like "modee" instead of "mode" or "vaIue" instead of "value" now fail fixture deserialization with a clear serde error rather than silently dropping the field. All family packet-runner tests pass except the pre-existing stats_packet_runner_passes failure (shapiro-wilk bug, orthogonal to this change).

title: [code-review] [HIGH] fsci-special lambertw_scalar(+inf) returns NaN instead of +inf
description: Review of dfa90ac (fix(fsci-special): lambertw_scalar initial guess for x < e) found a missed non-finite boundary. The new threshold fixes x=1 but still routes x=+inf into the asymptotic seed at crates/fsci-special/src/convenience.rs:2714: x.ln() - x.ln().ln(). For +inf this is inf - inf = NaN, so Halley's iteration returns NaN. SciPy 1.17.1 reports scipy.special.lambertw(float('inf')) == inf+0j, and the richer real path in crates/fsci-special/src/elliptic.rs has the same issue in its large-x seed. Add explicit +inf handling and boundary tests for the scalar convenience wrapper and tensor lambertw path.
close_reason: Fixed: real Lambert W scalar and tensor paths now return +inf for +inf, with convenience and tensor regression tests.

title: [nb5gj sub] fsci-stats GenNorm method-of-moments fit
description: Slice of frankenscipy-nb5gj. GenNorm(beta) standardized has Var = Γ(3/β) / Γ(1/β). Solve for β via bisection on β ∈ (0.1, 50): the variance is monotone decreasing in β (β=1 → Var=2 (Laplace), β=2 → Var=1 (Gaussian), β→∞ → Var=1/3 (uniform)). Add metamorphic relation: fitted distribution variance matches sample variance within tolerance.

title: GAP: fsci-linalg qr_delete / qr_insert / qr_multiply / qr_update (0/4 → 4/4)
description:
Per fgxx audit, fsci-linalg lacks all four QR-update primitives:
qr_delete, qr_insert, qr_multiply, qr_update. These are essential for
incremental least-squares (online regression, Kalman filter recursive
update, recursive RLS).

Scope:
- Implement qr_delete(Q, R, k) — delete row k, return updated (Q', R').
- Implement qr_insert(Q, R, u, k) — insert row u at position k.
- Implement qr_multiply(Q, R, c) — apply Q to a vector / matrix.
- Implement qr_update(Q, R, u, v) — rank-1 update: A + u·vᵀ.

All four can sit on top of the existing qr() decomposition. Reference
algorithms: Daniel/Gragg/Kaufman/Stewart "Reorthogonalization and
stable algorithms for updating the Gram-Schmidt QR factorization"
(1976) — same approach scipy uses.

Acceptance:
- Public functions in fsci_linalg with rustdoc that links the
  scipy.linalg counterpart.
- 4 unit tests per fn covering the happy path and a degenerate input.
- COVERAGE.md updated (qr_updates family flips from 0/4 to 4/4).
- Optional: a fixture case per fn in P2C-002.

Related: fgxx (parent audit), 5dr4 (oracle dispatcher coverage).
close_reason: Completed in 328e56a
