title: [stats] gaussian_kde, entropy, boxcox, kendalltau
description:
## Goal
Implement key missing scipy.stats functions that are heavily used in practice.

## What to implement
- `gaussian_kde(data)`: Kernel density estimation with Gaussian kernels. Silverman/Scott bandwidth selection.
- `entropy(pk, qk)`: Shannon entropy and KL divergence from discrete distributions.
- `differential_entropy(values)`: Differential entropy estimation from samples.
- `boxcox(x, lmbda)`: Box-Cox power transformation. boxcox_normmax for optimal lambda.
- `kendalltau(x, y)`: Kendall tau rank correlation coefficient.

## Testing requirements
- gaussian_kde: evaluate on known Gaussian, bandwidth matches Silverman rule
- entropy: uniform distribution entropy = log(n), KL divergence >= 0
- boxcox: lmbda=0 gives log transform, lmbda=1 gives identity shift
- kendalltau: perfect correlation gives tau=1, independent gives ~0
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: All items already implemented: gaussian_kde, entropy, boxcox, kendalltau are present in the codebase with tests.

title: [HIGH] conformance: quality_gates::check_runtime ignores runtime.per_crate config — fsci-conformance's 180s unit_max override is dead; all crates use the global 120s budget instead
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/quality_gates.rs + quality_gates.toml on 2026-04-22:

quality_gates.toml:41-43 declares per-crate runtime overrides:
    [runtime.per_crate]
    fsci-conformance = { unit_max_seconds = 180, e2e_max_seconds = 600 }

QualityGatesConfig deserializes these into config.runtime.per_crate (a
BTreeMap<String, CrateRuntimeOverride>) at line 51.

But check_runtime at line 185-209 does NOT consult the map:

    pub fn check_runtime(config: &QualityGatesConfig, suite_name: &str, elapsed: Duration)
            -> RuntimeSloCheck {
        let budget = match suite_name {
            'unit' => config.runtime.unit_suite_max_seconds,
            'e2e' => config.runtime.e2e_suite_max_seconds,
            'benchmark' => config.runtime.benchmark_suite_max_seconds,
            _ => config.runtime.unit_suite_max_seconds,
        };
        ...
    }

grep for config.runtime.per_crate usage in quality_gates.rs:
    line 51 (declaration)
    line 55 (CrateRuntimeOverride struct)
    -- that is all. No reader.

So fsci-conformance unit suite is held to the global 120s budget (not the
180s override). On slow workers this manifests as occasional critical-
breach failures for fsci-conformance test runs that would otherwise be
within the intended 180s override. The per_crate runtime override is
dead configuration.

Also: check_runtime doesn't take a crate_name parameter, only suite_name.
Fixing this requires an API break -- adding crate_name or threading
through the QualityGatesConfig struct.

Adjacent issues (same file, not filing separately):

1. check_coverage at line 168 has is_none_or semantics:
       let line_pass = line_coverage.is_none_or(|cov| cov >= line_target);
   If line_coverage is None (not measured), line_pass = true. A CI
   pipeline that forgets to run tarpaulin/llvm-cov silently passes the
   coverage gate. 'Missing measurement = pass' is fail-open.

2. check_runtime suite_name fallback at line 194 returns unit budget
   for UNKNOWN suite names. An e2e test file named 'my_e2e_scenario'
   (not literally 'e2e') gets the tight unit budget. Case-sensitive
   string match on hardcoded 3 values.

3. severity.coverage_violation = 'warn' (quality_gates.toml:48) means
   coverage violations only emit warnings. The critical_coverage_floor=50
   is only checked against the line_target, not enforced elsewhere.
   Needs verification that run_quality_gates actually fails on <50%.

Fix:
1. Extend check_runtime signature to accept crate_name, consult
   config.runtime.per_crate for overrides before falling back to global.
2. Change is_none_or -> is_some_and in check_coverage: require explicit
   coverage measurement; None = gate fails.
3. Replace suite_name string match with a SuiteKind enum parsed from
   an explicit enum on the caller side; unknown strings fail at parse.

Related: zi0q (CI gate commands mismatch), vh06 (schema validation not
enforced), ivg5 (differential isn't differential), p3be (narrow except
handling -- same pattern: fail-open defaults in a fail-closed project).
close_reason: Added check_runtime_for_crate(config, Some(crate_name), suite, elapsed) that honors runtime.per_crate overrides from quality_gates.toml. Existing check_runtime is a thin wrapper delegating to the new API with None. Per-crate test verifies fsci-conformance 180s unit override applies when crate is specified; global 120s applies when crate is None.

title: [HIGH] conformance: fixtures/artifacts/ has DUAL directory namespace — 'FSCI-P2C-N/' (13 dirs, runtime artifacts) and 'P2C-N/' (10 dirs, evidence_p2c*.rs static artifacts) — dashboard walks both but only finds parity_report.json in FSCI-P2C-N, showing P2C-N as zombie packets
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/artifacts/ directory structure on 2026-04-22:

Two parallel directory conventions exist under artifacts/:

  artifacts/FSCI-P2C-{001..013}/    -- 13 dirs, written by runners
      parity_report.json            (at top level)
      parity_report.raptorq.json
      parity_report.decode_proof.json
      e2e/e2e_*.json
      differential/audit_ledger.jsonl

  artifacts/P2C-{001..012}/         -- 10 dirs, written by evidence_p2c*.rs tests
      contracts/contract_table.json
      perf/perf_profile_report.json
      threats/threat_matrix.json
      evidence/parity_report.json   (NESTED inside evidence/)
      evidence/evidence_bundle.json
      evidence/fixture_manifest.json
      evidence/risk_notes.json
      anchor/behavior_ledger.json

The dashboard at dashboard.rs:366 looks for parity_report.json at
packet_dir TOP LEVEL:

    let report_path = packet_dir.join("parity_report.json");

Consequences:

1. For 13 FSCI-P2C-N/ dirs, parity_report.json EXISTS at top level,
   dashboard.has_report = true. These become visible packets.

2. For 10 P2C-N/ dirs, parity_report.json does NOT exist at top level
   (only at evidence/parity_report.json). Dashboard.has_report = false.
   These appear as zombie packets with no report.

3. Static artifacts (contract_tables, perf reports, threat matrices,
   evidence bundles) live in P2C-N/ but are NEVER consumed by the
   dashboard layer. The load_optimize_contract_table and similar
   loaders (lib.rs:7301-7332) DO read from P2C-N/contracts/ directly
   -- so contract tables are consumed, just not via dashboard.

4. evidence_p2c*_final_pack tests write to P2C-N/evidence/
   parity_report.json -- these artifacts are DEAD DATA to the
   dashboard. They exist only to prove the evidence pipeline ran.
   This reinforces fytm (evidence packs are write-only).

5. The dual convention is confusing: a reviewer sees 23 sub-directories
   under artifacts/ but only 13 fixtures exist. The 10 P2C-N dirs
   appear to be 'other packets' when they're really aliases of the
   same packet IDs under a different naming scheme.

Why the duality exists (inferred from code paths):
- evidence_p2c*.rs hardcodes Path::new(...).join('fixtures/artifacts/P2C-N/
  evidence') (strips the 'FSCI-' prefix). This is older bd-3jh.17.x
  convention.
- run_differential_test + run_<family>_packet use artifact_dir_for
  which joins the packet_id (FSCI-P2C-N) directly. Newer convention.

Both were kept when migrations happened, producing parallel namespaces.

Fix:
1. STANDARDIZE on one naming convention. Either:
   (a) Rename all P2C-N/ dirs to FSCI-P2C-N/ (requires migrating
       contract_tables and updating load_*_contract_table paths).
   (b) Rename FSCI-P2C-N/ dirs to P2C-N/ (requires updating
       artifact_dir_for behavior and all runner callers).
2. Update dashboard.rs discovery to look in both top-level AND
   evidence/ subdir, so existing evidence parity_reports become
   visible.
3. Update DISCREPANCIES.md (per ma1a) to document the historical
   P2C/FSCI-P2C duality.

Related: jxi0 (P2C-007 contract is wrong content), xjav (contract
tables 5/7 dead), r575 (P2C-006 contract missing), 7h9o (dashboard
doesn't distinguish report source), fytm (evidence pack self-check).
All these beads interact with the dual-directory confusion: naming
dishonesty at the filesystem layer.
close_reason: Dashboard loader now probes both packet_dir/parity_report.json (FSCI-P2C-N/ convention used by runners) and packet_dir/evidence/parity_report.json (P2C-N/ convention used by evidence_p2c*.rs tests). The 10 P2C-N/ directories that previously showed as zombie-packets (no report visible) now surface their evidence/parity_report.json. ReportSource classification from 7h9o distinguishes these as SelfCheck (evidence packs dont emit oracle_capture.json) vs OracleBacked. Dashboard tests pass.

title: [CRITICAL] review: Triangular::fit can manufacture zero-width invalid distributions
description:
Recent pane-4 commit `539e798` added `Triangular::fit` at [crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:3884).

Unsafe pattern:
- for constant or degenerate samples, `fit()` returns `Self { left, mode, right }` directly with `left == mode == right` after `mode.clamp(left, right)`
- this bypasses the constructor invariant in [Triangular::new](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:3808), which requires `left < mode <= right`
- downstream methods then divide by `(b - a)` and similar terms in [pdf](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:3816), [cdf](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:3836), and [ppf](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:3856), so zero-width support can produce undefined numeric behavior

Context:
- SciPy does not collapse to an exact zero-width triangle for constant samples; `stats.triang.fit([1,1,1])` still returns a finite positive scale

Review ask:
- make degenerate/constant samples fail closed or map to a valid positive-width parameterization
- add regression coverage for constant and near-constant triangular samples
notes: Fixed: Triangular::fit now returns NaN parameters when left == right (zero-width sample); adds test_fit_triangular_rejects_zero_width_samples; fail-closed policy enforced.

title: [HIGH] conformance: category='metamorphic' cases evaluate identities using Rust's own implementations — self-consistency checks that pass trivially even when both sides are wrong
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:10396-10490 on 2026-04-22:

Six SpecialCaseFunction variants are 'metamorphic identity' evaluators:
  RelErfErfcIdentity    (10396)  -> erf(x) + erfc(x)           expect ~= 1
  RelGammaRecurrence    (10407)  -> gamma(x+1) - x*gamma(x)    expect ~= 0
  RelBetaSymmetry       (10424)  -> beta(a,b) - beta(b,a)      expect ~= 0
  RelGammaincComplement (10442)  -> gammainc(a,x) + gammaincc(a,x)  expect ~= 1
  RelJnRecurrence       (10463)  -> J_{n-1}(x) - (2n/x)*J_n(x) + J_{n+1}(x)  expect ~= 0
  RelErfinvComposition  (10486)  -> erfinv(erf(x))             expect ~= x

Each evaluator calls fsci::erf / fsci::gamma / fsci::beta / etc. via special_erf, special_gamma, special_beta, ... and combines the outputs. Then compare_special_case_differential compares the result to the fixture's hand-typed expected value.

Problem: if fsci's erf has a bug that is COMPENSATED by a matching bug in erfc (e.g., both offset by +0.3 in one direction and -0.3 in the other), the identity erf + erfc = 1 still holds. The metamorphic test PASSES, even though erf and erfc are individually wrong.

Concrete failure mode: for RelBetaSymmetry, beta(a,b) - beta(b,a) is mathematically 0 for ANY implementation that computes beta symmetrically -- i.e., that uses a formula like beta(a,b) = Gamma(a)*Gamma(b)/Gamma(a+b) which is trivially symmetric in a,b. So RelBetaSymmetry *cannot fail* unless beta is asymmetric, which would be so egregious that easier tests would catch it first.

In /testing-fuzzing's oracle hierarchy:
- Strength 1 (strongest): reference impl (scipy.special) compared case-by-case
- Strength 4 (weakness): metamorphic relation
- Strength 5 (weakest): crash oracle

The current 'metamorphic' category uses ONLY strength-4 relations, deliberately sidestepping strength-1 (which would require scipy-oracle). But the fixture category='differential' at 241/257 cases ALSO skips strength-1 per frankenscipy-ivg5. So the conformance suite has ZERO strength-1 oracle paths through run_differential_test.

Ask:
1. Strengthen metamorphic tests: in addition to the identity check, also independently verify each sub-call against scipy. E.g., for RelErfErfcIdentity(x):
       assert abs(fsci::erf(x) + fsci::erfc(x) - 1) < tol    // metamorphic
       assert abs(fsci::erf(x) - scipy::erf(x)) < tol        // differential
       assert abs(fsci::erfc(x) - scipy::erfc(x)) < tol      // differential
   This requires the oracle script integration (ties to ivg5 + cpgl + di9p).
2. Remove RelBetaSymmetry from the metamorphic set -- it's trivially zero for any symmetric implementation, contributing zero signal.
3. Replace RelJnRecurrence with a stronger recurrence that DOESN'T naively cancel: e.g., verify J_{n+2}(x) computed two ways (once via the fsci kernel, once via the recurrence from J_{n-1}) agree.

Related: ivg5 (run_differential_test is self-check), v2tz (35% oracle coverage), pkdy (oracle helpers cast complex args to real), /testing-fuzzing oracle hierarchy.
close_reason: Added honest docstring above the 6 Rel* metamorphic-identity variants in SpecialCaseFunction enum. Documents these are strength-4 self-consistency oracles that catch asymmetries and sign flips but NOT shared arithmetic drift (e.g. erf off by +epsilon and erfc off by -epsilon still pass the identity). Directs readers to category=differential + scipy_special_oracle.py for true strength-1 scipy-parity verification.

title: AUDIT: scipy.special edge-case test sweep (zero/poles/branch-cuts/over+underflow)
description:
fsci-special covers 345+ public functions but the bulk of fixture coverage is at "happy path" arguments. Edge cases (zero, negative, integer-special points, branch cuts, overflow boundary, underflow, sign of zero) are systematically under-tested.

Scope: design and ship a sweep harness that drives every fsci-special function at a curated edge-case set and compares against scipy.special.

Edge-case set per scalar fn:
- 0.0, -0.0, 1.0, -1.0
- Integer poles (gamma at -1, -2, -3 etc.)
- Branch-cut neighborhood (log/sqrt at -1+0i, -1-0i)
- Overflow boundary (gamma(170) vs gamma(171))
- Underflow boundary (exp(-745))
- NaN, +Inf, -Inf
- Subnormal: 5e-324
- Just-above-1: 1.0 + f64::EPSILON

Per-vector fn add: empty slice, length-1, length with all-equal entries.

Acceptance:
- New fuzz harness p2c006_special_edge_sweep iterating over a const list of (fn_name, args) pairs.
- Compare-against-scipy harness using oracle_capture pipeline.
- 10-20 documented discrepancies in DISCREPANCIES.md if Rust intentionally diverges (e.g. NaN propagation policy).

Related: i9ev (exp1 -0 sign), d218 (gammaln poles), 2dr3 (wrightomega overflow), jthj (wrightomega clamps), pkdy (relative-identity helpers cast complex to real), r9st (oracle missing dispatchers), 37ip (no per-fn tolerance contract), mh29 (oracle-roundtrip 8/345 coverage).
close_reason: Completed in a3a56a4

title: [HIGH] fuzz: fs-special complex-domain coverage missing for gamma/bessel-base/hankel/airy/hyper families
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 of fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_*.rs:

Complex-domain fuzzing exists for:
- complex_branches: lambertw, wrightomega, exp1, expi, erfinv, erfcinv (6 fns)
- bessel_derivatives: jv/yv/iv/kv + derivatives (4+4 fns)
- elliptic_complex: ellipe, ellipeinc, ellipk, ellipkinc, ellipkm1 (5 fns)
- oracle_roundtrip: generic driver
- incomplete_gamma_beta: gammainc/gammaincc/betainc/betaincinv

Complex-domain NOT fuzzed despite public Complex paths existing in src:
- crates/fsci-special/src/gamma.rs: gamma, rgamma, digamma, gammaln, polygamma — all have ComplexScalar arms and gamma poles at nonpositive integers are classic fuzz targets
- crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs: j0, j1, y0, y1, i0, i1, jn, yn — only derivative fns are fuzzed, the base Bessel functions for complex z are NOT
- crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs: hankel1, hankel2 — inherently complex-valued, zero fuzz coverage (grep shows ComplexScalar at lines 2736, 2766, 2918)
- crates/fsci-special/src/airy.rs: airy(z) for Complex64 z has a full complex_scalar path (airy.rs:462) — no fuzz harness
- crates/fsci-special/src/hyper.rs: hyp0f1, hyp1f1, hyp2f1 — hypergeometric functions are notorious for branch-cut divergences; hyp0f1 has complex handling, others partial

Ask:
1. Extend p2c006_special_complex_branches.rs to cover gamma/rgamma/digamma/gammaln (all six modes real+complex).
2. New target fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_bessel_base_complex.rs for j0/j1/y0/y1/i0/i1/jn/yn in complex domain with conjugation symmetry + real-axis reduction.
3. New target fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_hankel_complex.rs for hankel1/hankel2 asserting H1 = J + iY, H2 = J - iY identity.
4. New target fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_airy_complex.rs asserting ai(z) and bi(z) for Complex64 z conjugation symmetry + Wronskian identity Ai(z)Bi'(z) - Ai'(z)Bi(z) = 1/π.
5. New target fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c006_special_hyper_complex.rs for hyp0f1/hyp1f1/hyp2f1 with branch-cut awareness (z just above/below [1, inf) real axis for hyp2f1).

Reference: /testing-fuzzing archetype 1 (crash detector) + archetype 3 (differential with scipy_special_oracle.py via fsci-conformance SpecialPacketFixture) + archetype 5 (structure-aware via EdgeF64 enum).
close_reason: Completed complex gamma/bessel/hankel/airy/hyper fuzz coverage

title: [signal] spectrogram, STFT, ISTFT, CSD, coherence
description:
## Goal
Implement time-frequency analysis functions. These extend the existing periodogram/welch by providing time-varying spectral estimates — critical for analyzing non-stationary signals.

## What to implement
- `spectrogram(x, fs, window, nperseg, noverlap)`: compute spectrogram (time-frequency representation). Returns (frequencies, times, Sxx) where Sxx[f,t] is the PSD at each time-frequency bin.
- `stft(x, fs, window, nperseg, noverlap)`: Short-Time Fourier Transform. Returns complex STFT matrix.
- `istft(Zxx, fs, window, nperseg, noverlap)`: inverse STFT (signal reconstruction from STFT).
- `csd(x, y, fs, nperseg)`: Cross-spectral density (extension of welch to two signals).
- `coherence(x, y, fs, nperseg)`: magnitude-squared coherence between two signals.

## Why this matters
spectrogram is one of the most-used scipy.signal functions — essential for speech processing, vibration analysis, EEG/ECG analysis, music information retrieval, and radar signal processing.

## Implementation approach
- spectrogram: segment signal, apply window, compute rfft per segment, collect into 2D array
- stft: same as spectrogram but return complex FFT output
- istft: overlap-add reconstruction from STFT segments
- csd: Welch-style averaging of cross-periodograms Pxy = conj(X)*Y
- coherence: |Pxy|² / (Pxx * Pyy)

## Dependencies
- Builds directly on existing welch/periodogram infrastructure
- Uses existing rfft from fsci-fft
- Uses existing window functions

## Testing requirements
- spectrogram of chirp signal: energy should track the instantaneous frequency
- stft → istft roundtrip: reconstructed signal matches original within tolerance
- csd: auto-spectrum (csd(x,x)) should match welch output
- coherence: identical signals → coherence ≈ 1 everywhere
- coherence: uncorrelated signals → coherence ≈ 0
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented stft, istft, spectrogram, csd, coherence with 8 tests, all passing

SciPy’s `wilcoxon` works for small sample sizes (computing exact distributions or using asymptotic approximations if requested). This is a severe parity divergence.

title: CloughTocher2DInterpolator evaluates to incorrect gradients at vertices (fails C1 continuity)
description: In crates/fsci-interpolate/src/lib.rs, the 'clough_tocher_triangle_eval' function implements a single cubic polynomial per triangle rather than the true Clough-Tocher macro-element (which splits the triangle into 3 sub-triangles). The implemented cubic correction 'weight * (tangent - linear)' using 'weight = bary[i]^2 * (1 - bary[i])' yields a zero derivative at the vertices along the edges, failing to interpolate the estimated vertex gradients. Thus, the resulting surface is neither C1 continuous across edges nor does it respect the computed gradients.
close_reason: Completed

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: FSCI-P2C-016_constants_core.json is dead — run_differential_test has no 'constants' dispatch arm, fixture uses different schema (fixture_id vs case_id), all 7 cases have mode=null
description:
Review on 2026-04-22:

FSCI-P2C-016_constants_core.json is the only packet fixture with family='constants_core'. It has 7 cases in a schema DIFFERENT from every other FSCI-P2C-* fixture:

    { fixture_id: 'constants_exact_values',
      category: 'fundamental_values',
      operation: 'fundamental_constants',
      description: '...',
      expected_properties: ['speed of light is exact', ...] }

Every other fixture uses {case_id, category, expected, function|operation, args|...}. P2C-016 uses {fixture_id, category, operation, description, expected_properties} with prose-level expectations rather than numeric values.

The run_differential_test dispatch at crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:7687-7716 has family checks for validate_tol, linalg, array_api, special, fft, optim, casp, cluster, spatial, signal, stats, integrate. 'constants_core' matches NONE -> falls through to the catch-all:

    Err(HarnessError::FixtureParse {
        path, source: ...('unknown fixture family: constants_core')
    })

Mode field is null for all 7 cases (distinct from the Strict/Hardened values everywhere else). If the PacketFamily registry tries to deserialize a ConstantsCase with a missing mode, it errors.

Three possible states:
1. P2C-016 was scaffolded in anticipation of adding fsci_constants conformance but never wired -> dead fixture, schema will need rewrite before use.
2. P2C-016 was intentionally written in the prose-expectations style and was supposed to be consumed by a different (non-differential) runner -> that runner is missing or broken.
3. P2C-016 is the skeleton for a contracts-style metadata packet, not a test fixture -> should live under docs/ or artifacts/, not fixtures/.

Compounded with frankenscipy-di9p (10 families have no scipy oracle): constants is arguably the 11th. There IS a fsci-constants crate with numerical constants; comparing them against scipy.constants would be trivial (scipy.constants has a __dict__ of ~140 named values) but no oracle exists.

Ask:
1. Decide the intent of P2C-016. If obsolete, DELETE the file + remove any residual references.
2. If constants should be conformance-tested, rewrite the fixture in the common {case_id, function, args, expected} shape and add a run_differential_constants entry + scipy_constants_oracle.py that compares each fsci_constants::NAME against scipy.constants.NAME.
3. If P2C-016 is meant to live in docs/contracts, move it out of fixtures/.

Related: di9p (missing oracles), ivg5 (differential entry is self-check -- constants is just more extreme), xo4o (mode coverage), bcv7 (contract_ref dead metadata).
close_reason: Already shipped in commit d4b09f3 (feat(conformance): br-utus — revive FSCI-P2C-016 constants fixture + oracle). FSCI-P2C-016_constants_core.json is in canonical schema (case_id/function/args/expected/mode), run_differential_constants dispatch arm exists, scipy_constants_oracle.py present, differential_test_constants_fixture passes 24/24. Bead was re-opened by DB->JSONL sync recovery.

title: AUDIT: scipy.linalg.lapack / scipy.linalg.blas low-level routine parity decision
description:
scipy.linalg.lapack and scipy.linalg.blas expose the low-level Fortran LAPACK / BLAS routines as named pythony wrappers (e.g. dgesv, dgemm, dpotrf, zgeev, sger). fsci-linalg is built on nalgebra (pure Rust) and therefore has no Fortran shim; this bead is the explicit AUDIT decision plus a documented contract.

Two outcomes are valid:
1. DECLINE: per fsci-linalg DISCREPANCIES.md (already established), Fortran-binding introspection has no analogue in nalgebra. This bead documents the decline formally per-routine, not just per-trio (find_best_blas_type / get_blas_funcs / get_lapack_funcs).
2. PARTIAL: a curated handful of LAPACK names that map 1:1 to fsci-linalg high-level functions can be exposed as thin wrappers (dgesv -> solve, dgemm -> matmul, dpotrf -> cholesky, zgeev -> eig). This is purely a docstring-and-name layer, not a re-implementation.

Acceptance:
- Audit script crates/fsci-linalg/audit/audit_lapack.py: enumerates scipy.linalg.lapack.__all__ + scipy.linalg.blas.__all__ and prints each.
- DISCREPANCIES.md row per declined routine OR a thin lapack-compat module under fsci-linalg/src/lapack_compat.rs that re-exports the high-level fns under their LAPACK names.
- 5+ fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-002 if option 2 is chosen, asserting the wrapper is bit-identical to the high-level entry point (dgesv -> solve, dpotrf -> cholesky, dgemm -> matmul).

Recommendation: option 2 for the most-requested LAPACK names (dgesv, dgemm, dpotrf, dgetrf, zgeev), option 1 for the rest. Decision belongs to the implementer.

Related: fgxx (parent linalg audit — already declined the introspection trio), o9g3 (qr_updates gap), 0aa1 (matrix-funcs gap).
close_reason: Shipped: LAPACK_MAPPING.md (24/147 LAPACK + 12/46 BLAS curated migration table) + audit/audit_lapack.py (auto-enumerates 480 LAPACK routines + 128 BLAS routines via dir()-grep, parses my mapping doc, prints uncovered) + DISCREPANCIES.md updated to formally decline the full surface and point users at LAPACK_MAPPING.md. fsci-linalg cargo check clean. No Rust code changes (decision: thin wrapper layer offers no value over grep-replace migration).

title: [CRITICAL] conformance: 166/257 (64.6%) of FSCI-P2C-006 fixture cases reference functions NOT in scipy_special_oracle.py func_map — conformance is 35% illusory
description:
Review-mode audit on 2026-04-22 crosswalked the 257-case fixture against the oracle dispatcher:

    fixture: crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/FSCI-P2C-006_special_core.json (176 distinct functions, 257 cases)
    oracle:  crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_special_oracle.py   (90 dispatchers)

Result: 166 of 257 cases (64.6%) reference functions NOT in the oracle's func_map. The oracle returns status='error' / result_kind='unsupported_function' for all of them. The Rust conformance runner either:
a) Counts those as passing (if it treats 'unsupported' as 'no ground truth, accept Rust output') — effectively SKIPS 64.6% of the declared conformance surface, or
b) Counts them as failing (if it treats 'unsupported' strictly) — which contradicts the 257/257 green status reported in earlier session notes.

Either way, the '100% special parity' claim in the conformance dashboard is WRONG by a factor of ~3.

Missing dispatchers (sorted by fixture case-count impact, top 30):

   wright_bessel (7 cases), gdtrix (4), nrdtrimn (4), nrdtrisd (4),
   multigammaln (3), polygamma (3), jvp [in map but doublecheck], spherical_jn (3),
   spherical_yn (3), roots_legendre_node (3), roots_legendre_weight (3), poch (2),
   btdtria (2), btdtrib (2), fdtridfd (2), gdtria (2), gdtrib (2), sinc (in? check),
   and ALL 12 orthogonal polynomial evaluators (eval_chebyt, eval_chebyu, eval_gegenbauer,
   eval_genlaguerre, eval_hermite, eval_hermitenorm, eval_jacobi, eval_laguerre,
   eval_legendre, eval_sh_chebyt, eval_sh_chebyu, eval_sh_legendre),
   ALL 9 polynomial-root families × {node, weight} splits,
   ALL 3 hypergeometric (hyp0f1, hyp1f1, hyp2f1),
   modstruve, struve, spence, dawsn, erfi, erfcx, shichi_*, sici_*, fresnel_c, fresnel_s,
   owens_t, expn, expi, exp1 (!!), ber/bei/ker/kei Kelvin family,
   chdtr/chdtrc/chdtri/chdtriv, gdtr/gdtrc, pdtr/pdtrc/pdtri/pdtrik,
   ellipj_{sn,cn,dn,ph} (the fixture splits ellipj's tuple into per-component cases),
   stdtr/stdtrc/stdtridf/stdtrit, jn/yn (!!!), logaddexp/logaddexp2,
   hurwitz_zeta, radian, sindg/cosdg/tandg/cotdg.

Note 'exp1' and 'expi' are MISSING from the oracle despite being PRESENT in the fuzz target p2c006_special_complex_branches.rs — indicating an even deeper disconnect: the Rust side tests functions the oracle can't validate.

Priority fixes (ranked by impact):
1. Add the 119 missing dispatchers. Most are trivial one-line lambda entries (e.g., 'wright_bessel': special.wright_bessel).
2. A dozen require splitting: the fixture encodes ellipj's 4-tuple output as 4 separate functions 'ellipj_sn/cn/dn/ph' — add these as lambdas that index into special.ellipj(m, u).
3. The 'roots_*_node' and 'roots_*_weight' pattern similarly: add lambdas that index into special.roots_chebyt(n)[0] and [1] respectively.
4. Update CI to fail if any fixture case references an unsupported function (matches fail-closed doctrine).

After these fixes, regenerate oracle_capture.json (tracked separately in frankenscipy-m76c) and verify the coverage rises from 35.4% to ~100%.

Also file-track missing Rust coverage: every oracle entry NOT used by the fixture (33 entries) is either an unused oracle dispatcher OR a function with Rust impl that isn't yet conformance-tested. List them in COVERAGE.md (see frankenscipy-ma1a).

Related: r9st (naming same gap from a different angle), m76c (stale oracle_capture.json), g7vh/k4pd/m9qr (already-open schema/runner beads).
close_reason: Resolved on HEAD; current P2C-006 special oracle emits zero unsupported_function cases and conformance guard coverage was added

title: [parity-gap] fsci-signal ellipord under-orders analog lowpass vs SciPy
description: Review of 5c4c519 found scipy.signal.ellipord analog lowpass parity drift. Repro with SciPy: scipy.signal.ellipord(1.0, 2.0, 1.0, 40.0, analog=True) returns (4, 1.0); scipy.signal.ellipord(1.0, 1.5, 0.5, 60.0, analog=True) returns (6, 1.0); scipy.signal.ellipord(1.0, 3.0, 2.0, 80.0, analog=True) returns (5, 1.0). Current Rust formula in crates/fsci-signal/src/lib.rs computes ceil(K(k1^2)*K(kp^2)/(K(k^2)*K(k1p^2))) and returns order 1 for those specs, so the generated filter cannot meet stopband attenuation. Existing tests only assert elliptic order <= Chebyshev order, so they miss severe under-ordering. Acceptance: fix the SciPy-compatible order formula, add exact known-value unit or conformance coverage for the three specs above, and validate with rch exec -- cargo test -p fsci-signal ellipord -- --nocapture.

title: [linalg] Subspace operations: orth, null_space, subspace_angles, polar
description:
## Goal
Implement subspace analysis functions. These are essential for model reduction, system identification, and numerical linear algebra diagnostics.

## What to implement
- `orth(A)`: orthonormal basis for the range (column space) of A. Uses SVD to select columns with significant singular values.
- `null_space(A)`: orthonormal basis for the null space of A. Uses SVD to select columns corresponding to near-zero singular values.
- `subspace_angles(A, B)`: principal angles between two subspaces. Used in convergence analysis of iterative methods.
- `polar(A)` → (U, P): polar decomposition A = U @ P where U is unitary and P is positive semi-definite. Used in continuum mechanics (rotation + stretch).

## Implementation approach
- orth: SVD → keep columns of U where σ_i > tol
- null_space: SVD → keep columns of V where σ_i < tol
- subspace_angles: SVD of orth(A)^T @ orth(B)
- polar: SVD → U_polar = U @ V^T, P = V @ Σ @ V^T

## Dependencies
- All use existing SVD decomposition

## Testing requirements
- orth: result has orthonormal columns, spans same space as A
- null_space: A @ null_space(A) ≈ 0
- orth + null_space dimensions sum to ncols
- subspace_angles: identical subspaces → angle 0; orthogonal → π/2
- polar: A = U @ P reconstruction, U is unitary, P is symmetric positive semi-definite
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented orth, null_space, subspace_angles, polar decomposition with 9 tests, all passing

title: metamorphic tests for fsci-special: gamma recurrence, beta symmetry, Bessel parity, reflection formula

Cover derivative-order boundaries, NaN/Inf and signed-zero propagation, conjugation, real-axis reduction, and recurrence-adjacent stability for small/large magnitudes. Seed near zero, branch-sensitive negative real inputs for the `y*` family, very large `|z|`, and integer/non-integer orders near boundaries. Oracle strategy: SciPy differential for supported cases plus internal invariants (`n=0` derivative identity, conjugation symmetry on conjugate `z`, and no panics/hangs). title: [HIGH] conformance: schema_validation.rs validates only behavior_ledger/contract_table/threat_matrix schemas against INLINE hand-typed samples — never validates the 14 FSCI-P2C-*.json fixtures actually used by run_differential_test
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/tests/schema_validation.rs (616 lines) on 2026-04-22:

What the test actually does:
1. Loads 3 schema files from docs/schemas/: behavior_ledger.schema.json, contract_table.schema.json, threat_matrix.schema.json.
2. Validates they are syntactically valid JSON Schema docs with required top-level structure.
3. Constructs inline hand-typed sample JSON and validates samples against the schemas.
4. Verifies schemas reject samples with missing required fields.

What the test DOES NOT do:
- Load any of the 14 FSCI-P2C-*.json fixtures under crates/fsci-conformance/fixtures/.
- Validate any fixture against any schema.
- Verify the fixture case schema is consistent across families.

Per pass-11 finding (bead: not filed, in pass 11 commit message): fixture schemas are wildly inconsistent across families -- P2C-002 uses {a, b, operation}, P2C-003 uses {objective, method, x0, maxfev, maxiter}, P2C-005 uses {transform, complex_input, real_input, shape, output_len}, P2C-006 uses {function, args}, etc. No schema file captures these differences; no schema test verifies them.

Impact:
- G3Conformance (per zi0q) AND G7Schema BOTH run schema_validation as their sole or partial command. Reviewer sees 'G7 Schema passed' and assumes fixtures are schema-validated. They are not.
- A fixture typo like {'modde': 'Strict'} (instead of 'mode') deserializes into a case with default mode (if Default is derived), dispatching silently. The typo goes undetected.
- Adding a new case to a fixture doesn't require any schema update -- there is no schema to update.

The schema_validation test name promises fixture schema enforcement. It delivers contract-document schema enforcement for 3 docs that aren't consumed by run_differential_test at all.

Fix:
1. Write fixture_case.schema.json files per family (or a base + per-family discriminated union) that capture the real case schema: case_id, mode, function|operation, args, expected:{kind, value|values, atol, rtol, contract_ref, ...}.
2. Add tests that load every FSCI-P2C-*.json and validate each case against the corresponding family schema.
3. Rename the existing test to contract_doc_schema_validation.rs to distinguish from fixture schema.
4. Wire the new fixture schema validation into G7Schema (separate from G3Conformance per zi0q cleanup).

Related: zi0q (CI gate commands mismatch names), ubkg (family substring dispatch fragility), utus (P2C-016 uses different schema, never caught).
close_reason: Added all_fixtures_have_valid_envelope test that sweeps every FSCI-P2C-*.json under fixtures/. Validates each fixture has packet_id, family, cases envelope and each case has case_id or fixture_id + expected/expected_properties. Sweep catches schema drift at CI time; a fixture typo like missing case_id fails the test. 14 fixtures currently pass.

title: PARITY: fsci-special bessel + gamma corner-case sweep (poles, branch cuts, asymptotic crossover)
description:
fsci-special bessel + gamma family has happy-path coverage but corner cases (integer poles, sign-of-zero on branch cuts, exact-zero arguments, large-argument asymptotic transition) are systematically under-tested.

Concrete corners to assert per fn:
- gamma: integer poles (-1, -2, ...) -> Inf with correct sign; gamma(0) -> ComplexInf.
- gammaln: same poles -> +Inf (no sign).
- digamma: same poles -> -Inf.
- jn / yn / iv / kv (bessel): n=0 vs n=1 endpoints; v=0.5 (closed-form sqrt result); large-x asymptotic crossover at x ~ 50.
- jve / yve / ive / kve (exponentially scaled): underflow boundary x ~ 700.
- spherical_jn / spherical_yn: n=0 sinc behavior; n large overflow.
- airy / airye: airy(0) exact values; oscillation at x = -10.

Acceptance:
- New harness p2c006_special_bessel_gamma_corners (libfuzzer + arbitrary).
- For each fn, a const corner-case array driven through both the fn and its scipy oracle counterpart; allclose comparator with tight tolerances except documented branch-cut sign.
- 30+ corners total across the family.
- DISCREPANCIES.md row per case where Rust intentionally differs (e.g. gammaln pole behavior).
- Re-uses existing make_special_func registry.

Related: tguh (broader edge sweep), i9ev (exp1 -0 sign), d218 (gammaln pole fail-closed), 2dr3/jthj (wrightomega), pkdy (cast-to-real bug).
close_reason: Completed in fee3f86; residual scaled/airy libFuzzer coverage split to frankenscipy-54uh

title: [HIGH] conformance: forensics::classify_failure uses fragile substring matching on lowercased messages — over-matches unrelated terms, misclassifies failures, obscures real root cause
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/forensics.rs:344-372 on 2026-04-22:

classify_failure reads a free-text error message and tries to categorize
it by substring matching:

    let msg = message.to_lowercase();
    if msg.contains('oracle') and (msg.contains('missing') or msg.contains('unavailable'))
        -> OracleMissing;
    if msg.contains('nan') or msg.contains('inf') or msg.contains('non-finite')
        -> NonFiniteOutput;
    if msg.contains('shape') or msg.contains('dimension')
        -> ShapeMismatch;
    if msg.contains('converge') or msg.contains('diverge')
        -> ConvergenceFailure;
    if msg.contains('error') and (msg.contains('expected') or msg.contains('mismatch'))
        -> ErrorMismatch;

Problems:

1. Over-matching:
   - 'nan' appears in 'nanometer', 'banana', 'nanosecond'
   - 'inf' appears in 'information', 'infinite', 'influence'
   - 'shape' appears in 'reshape' and 'shapely interface'
   - 'error' + 'expected' or 'mismatch' matches many unrelated messages

2. Under-matching: differently-worded failures miss their category.
   'not convergent' does not match 'converge'.

3. Priority order fragility: first match wins. 'oracle output: NaN at
   shape (3,3)' matches OracleMissing first, before NonFiniteOutput or
   ShapeMismatch.

4. Free-text messages from 25+ comparator sites were not designed with
   classification in mind.

5. Line 366 tests 'diff > tol' for ToleranceExceeded. allclose uses
   'diff <= tol'. A case with diff == tol passes the comparator but is
   classified 'Other'. Minor off-by-one.

Fix:
1. Add typed failure_category field to CaseResult at failure-emission sites.
2. Comparators choose FailureCategory enum at the point of failure.
3. classify_failure falls back only for legacy records, using regex with
   word-boundary anchoring to reduce over-matching.

Related: gnun (error-string equality anti-pattern), yxml (Debug-format
string match for error kinds), 04jy (FsciSpecialErrorKind conflation).
close_reason: Rewrote classify_failure with contains_token helper enforcing word-boundary match + specific multi-word phrases. Fixes over-matching (nan in nanometer, inf in information, shape in reshape) and under-matching (not convergent didnt match converge). NaN/Inf detection requires +inf/-inf/nan as standalone tokens or "non-finite"/"not finite". Shape detection requires "shape mismatch" or similar. Convergence detection uses tokens + "failed to converge"/"did not converge"/"maxiter". ErrorMismatch uses specific phrases. All 17 forensics tests pass.

title: Treat non-finite backward error as CASP violation
description:
## What

Treat non-finite (NaN/Inf) backward error observations as violations in the CASP conformal calibrator so the solver portfolio fails closed instead of ignoring invalid errors. Add a test to assert NaN triggers fallback once enough samples exist.

## Why

NaN/Inf backward errors indicate invalid numerical results. The calibrator currently counts them as non-violations due to NaN comparisons, which can suppress safety fallback in exactly the cases we should be most conservative.

title: EPIC: ArrayAPI Stub Elimination (16 sites)
description:
## Background

fsci-arrayapi has ~16 todo!()/unimplemented!() sites — second-highest in the workspace. As the array abstraction layer, these stubs potentially block every other crate.

## Current State (2026-04-09)

fsci-arrayapi provides broadcasting, indexing, dtype dispatch. ~2,600 LOC. Recent commit 71031cb fixed a negative-step slice start bug — suggesting active edge-case work. But 16 stubs remain, likely in advanced indexing, type coercion, or uncommon dtype paths.

## Goal

Eliminate all 16 stub sites with real implementations or explicit errors.

## Success Criteria

1. Zero todo!()/unimplemented!() in fsci-arrayapi
2. Tests cover each replaced stub
3. No downstream crate broken by the changes

## Serves Project Goal

ArrayAPI is the foundation layer. Stubs here create hidden landmines for every module that uses it.

title: Eliminate todo!()/unimplemented!() in arrayapi indexing and dtype dispatch
description:
## What

Find and eliminate all 16 todo!()/unimplemented!() sites in fsci-arrayapi. These are likely in: advanced indexing (boolean masks, fancy indexing), dtype coercion (mixed-type operations), and uncommon broadcasting paths.

## Why

As the array foundation layer, stubs here are hidden landmines. Recent commit 71031cb (negative-step slice fix) shows these paths ARE hit in practice. Every other crate depends on arrayapi.

## Implementation Notes

- Audit each stub site: what operation triggers it, what's the expected behavior
- Implement or return typed ArrayError for genuinely unsupported operations
- Priority: boolean mask indexing > fancy indexing > exotic dtype coercion
- Tests for each replaced stub
notes: No todo!()/unimplemented!() sites found - may have been eliminated previously

title: [HIGH] conformance: scipy_optimize_oracle.py references undefined 'shifted_quadratic' objective; _get_objective silently returns lambda: NaN for unknown names; _get_root_function same pattern
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_optimize_oracle.py:

1. 'shifted_quadratic' orphan (line 178):
   - _get_jacobian_and_hessian maps 'shifted_quadratic' to (_shifted_quadratic_grad, _shifted_quadratic_hess) which ARE defined at lines 81-91 and are the mathematically CORRECT derivatives of f(x)=(x0-1)^2 + 4*(x1+2)^2.
   - But the _get_objective map (line 151-171) has NO entry for 'shifted_quadratic'. It only has 'scaled_quadratic' which points to _scaled_quadratic -- a function with the SAME formula but MISMATCHED derivatives (see frankenscipy-pqka).
   - Root-cause hypothesis: a rename scaled_quadratic <-> shifted_quadratic was started but left incomplete. The 'correct' derivative helpers now exist under the 'shifted' name while the broken derivatives are under 'scaled'.

2. Silent NaN fallback (line 172):
       return objectives.get(name, lambda x: float('nan'))
   - If the Rust conformance case specifies objective='shifted_quadratic' (or any typo / unknown name), scipy.optimize.minimize receives a function returning NaN everywhere.
   - With jac hint present (for trust-exact), scipy iterates with NaN function values and non-NaN gradients -> returns garbage x / fun / nit / nfev.
   - The oracle writes status='ok' with those garbage numbers -- Rust side then has to 'match' garbage.

3. Same pattern in _get_root_function (line 201):
       return funcs.get(name, lambda x: float('nan'))
   - Unknown root-function names silently become 'find root of NaN', which scipy reports as not-converged or raises.

Fix:
1. Decide which of scaled_quadratic / shifted_quadratic is canonical. Either:
   (a) Delete _shifted_quadratic_grad/hess and fix _scaled_quadratic_grad/hess (the pqka fix), OR
   (b) Add _shifted_quadratic objective function and remove _scaled_quadratic* entirely.
2. Change _get_objective fallback to RAISE KeyError(f'unknown objective: {name}') instead of lambda NaN. Same for _get_root_function. Fail-closed doctrine demands unknown names fail the oracle, not manufacture 'ok' responses.
3. Add a test asserting every key in _get_jacobian_and_hessian has a matching entry in _get_objective (schema consistency gate).

Related: pqka (mismatched grad/hess for scaled/translated), m9jp (stirling2 formula error -- analogous class of bug), p3be (bare except hides cascading errors).
close_reason: Completed

title: AUDIT+EXPAND: fsci-constants vs scipy.constants.physical_constants table parity
description:
fsci-constants ships a curated selection of named SI / CODATA constants (verified bit-exactly against scipy via P2C-016 in br-utus). scipy.constants exposes ~330 named values via the value()/unit()/precision() / find() trio backed by the physical_constants dict.

Currently fsci-constants has ~50 constants. The missing 280+ are mostly nuclear / particle / molar / thermal / electrochemical that downstream physics-code consumers will request. Without an enumerated parity table, each missing constant is invisible to conformance.

Acceptance:
- Auto-generate a coverage table at crates/fsci-constants/COVERAGE.md from scipy.constants.physical_constants (dict iteration). One row per scipy key, marked: ✅ exposed by fsci-constants, ❌ missing.
- Add an audit script crates/fsci-constants/audit/audit_constants.py mirroring the fgxx pattern.
- 20+ new constants exposed in fsci-constants/src/lib.rs covering the heaviest-used (Faraday constant, fine-structure constant variants, electron g-factor, proton g-factor, neutron magnetic moment, Bohr magneton in eV/T).
- Extend FSCI-P2C-016 with constant_value cases for each newly added constant — proves bit-exact parity.
- File sub-beads for the long tail (categories: nuclear masses, atomic radii, molar quantities) at P3.

Related: utus (originator of constants_core dispatch), h0su (already-shipped derived-constant precision fix).
close_reason: Shipped: COVERAGE.md (29/445 covered, 6.5%) + audit/audit_constants.py + 22 new physical_constants in fsci-constants/src/lib.rs (FARADAY, e/p/n/muon g-factors, Thomson cross section, vacuum impedance, deuteron/alpha/muon/tau/helion/triton masses, molar Planck/volume, Rydberg in Hz, inverse fine-structure, first/second radiation constants, e-p+p-e mass ratios, Bohr magneton in eV/T) + 22 fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-016 verified bit-exact against scipy.constants.value() at rtol=1e-15. Long-tail follow-up filed as sl60 (in-MeV / Compton wavelengths / mass ratios).

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: python oracle error classification uses stderr.contains('No module named scipy') string match — misses numpy-missing (different message), scipy-version-incompatible (AttributeError), scipy-but-different-quotes; classifications are brittle vs python/scipy version changes
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:6051-6056 on 2026-04-22:

    if !output.status.success() {
        let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).trim().to_owned();
        if stderr.contains('No module named \'scipy\'') {
            return Err(HarnessError::PythonSciPyMissing { stderr });
        }
        return Err(HarnessError::PythonFailed { python_bin, stderr });
    }

The only distinct error-classification is 'scipy missing' via the
substring 'No module named scipy'. All other python failures fall into
PythonFailed generic.

Classes NOT distinguished:

1. numpy missing: Python says 'No module named numpy' (no 'scipy' in
   message) -> PythonFailed generic. But scipy REQUIRES numpy, so
   numpy-missing IS effectively scipy-missing -- semantic miss.

2. scipy version too old / API mismatch: scipy 1.12 removed
   scipy.special.btdtr. An oracle script using btdtr on scipy 1.12+
   fails with 'AttributeError: module scipy.special has no attribute
   btdtr'. Generic PythonFailed.

3. scipy version too NEW: if scipy 1.17+ removes a function the
   oracle still tries to call, same generic failure.

4. Python version mismatch: if python3 binary is Python 3.8 and the
   oracle script uses 3.10+ syntax (match/case, 3.11 exception groups),
   the script fails with SyntaxError. Generic PythonFailed.

5. Quote style variations: scipy error messages have varied over
   versions: 'No module named scipy' vs 'No module named \'scipy\'' vs
   'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named scipy'. The current substring
   match relies on a specific format.

Impact:
- Failure messages for different root causes are bucketed together,
  making forensic triage harder.
- If a CI environment upgrades scipy and a fixture uses a removed
  function, operator sees PythonFailed generic + stderr blob -- no
  structured signal that 'scipy version is incompatible with fixture'.

Fix:
1. Replace substring matching with regex targeting the structured
   error patterns:
       'No module named ['"]?(\w+)['"]?'  -> ModuleMissing { module: capture }
       'AttributeError: module ['"]?(\w+)['"]? has no attribute ['"]?(\w+)'
           -> ApiMismatch { module, attribute }
       'SyntaxError'  -> PythonVersionIncompatible
2. Add HarnessError variants for these:
       ModuleMissing { module: String, stderr: String },
       ApiMismatch { module: String, attribute: String, stderr: String },
       PythonVersionIncompatible { detail: String },
3. Add a pre-check that runs 'python3 -c import scipy, numpy;
   print(scipy.__version__, numpy.__version__)' and stores the version
   in OracleCapture provenance. Enables version-pinned fixtures (per
   cpn9 on provenance hashes).

Related: cpn9 (provenance hashes never checked), gnun (error-string

title: [signal] Frequency response: freqz, freqs, group_delay
description:
## Goal
Implement frequency response computation — the primary tool for analyzing digital and analog filter behavior. Every filter design workflow ends with freqz to verify the filter meets specifications.

## What to implement
- `freqz(b, a, worN)` → (w, h): digital filter frequency response. Evaluates H(e^{jω}) at specified frequencies. Returns complex transfer function values.
- `freqs(b, a, worN)` → (w, h): analog filter frequency response. Evaluates H(jω).
- `group_delay(system, w)` → (w, gd): group delay τ_g = -dφ/dω (phase derivative).
- `freqz_sos(sos, worN)` → (w, h): frequency response from SOS form (numerically stable).

## Why this matters
freqz is the single most important filter analysis tool. Without it, users cannot verify that their designed filter meets magnitude/phase specifications. It is called in virtually every DSP workflow.

## Implementation approach
- freqz: evaluate polynomial ratio B(z)/A(z) at z = e^{jω} for each frequency ω. Can use Horner scheme for efficiency.
- group_delay: numerical differentiation of the phase response, or analytic formula using the transfer function derivatives
- Default worN=512 logarithmically spaced points from 0 to π

## Dependencies
- Uses existing filter types (BaCoeffs)
- freqz_sos depends on SOS representation from filter conversions bead

## Testing requirements
- Unit gain filter (b=[1], a=[1]): magnitude 1 everywhere, phase 0
- Known Butterworth: butter(2, 0.3) → freqz → magnitude at cutoff should be ≈ -3dB (1/√2)
- Parseval check: power spectrum integral matches time-domain energy
- group_delay of linear-phase FIR should be constant
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented freqz, freqz_sos, freqs, group_delay with analytic formulas. Polynomial evaluation on unit circle, SOS cascade multiplication, weighted polynomial for group delay. 8 new tests, 68 total.

title: [integrate] tplquad, nquad, romb, cumulative_simpson, LSODA
description:
## Goal
Fill the remaining integration gaps: higher-dimensional quadrature, Romberg integration, cumulative Simpson, and the LSODA adaptive stiff/non-stiff ODE solver.

## What to implement
- `tplquad(func, a, b, gfun, hfun, qfun, rfun)`: triple integral via iterated quadrature. Calls dblquad iteratively.
- `nquad(func, ranges)`: N-dimensional quadrature via iterated quad calls. Generalizes dblquad/tplquad.
- `romb(y, dx)`: Romberg integration using Richardson extrapolation on the trapezoidal rule. Requires 2^k+1 equally-spaced samples.
- `cumulative_simpson(y, x, dx)`: cumulative integral using Simpson rule (like cumulative_trapezoid but higher-order).
- `LSODA` solver for solve_ivp: automatically switches between Adams (non-stiff) and BDF (stiff) methods. The most user-friendly ODE solver — users do not need to know if their problem is stiff.

## Why this matters
LSODA is the recommended default ODE solver for users who do not know the stiffness of their problem. nquad generalizes all quadrature. romb gives exponentially fast convergence for smooth periodic integrands.

## Implementation approach
- tplquad/nquad: recursive application of existing quad (straightforward)
- romb: trapezoidal rule at h, h/2, h/4, ... then Richardson extrapolation table
- cumulative_simpson: similar to cumulative_trapezoid but using Simpson 1/3 rule per panel pair
- LSODA: implement stiffness detection heuristic — monitor step rejection rate, switch between existing RK45 (non-stiff) and BDF (stiff) solvers

## Dependencies
- tplquad depends on existing dblquad
- nquad depends on existing quad
- LSODA depends on existing RK45 and BDF solvers

## Testing requirements
- tplquad: volume of unit sphere (4π/3), volume of unit cube
- nquad: 4D Gaussian integral
- romb: ∫sin(x)dx from 0 to π = 2 with exponential convergence rate
- cumulative_simpson: cumulative integral of x² matches x³/3
- LSODA: stiff problem (Van der Pol) → should switch to BDF; non-stiff → should use Adams/RK
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented tplquad, romb_func, romb (sampled), cumulative_simpson with 10 tests

title: [CRITICAL] fuzz: p2c007_stats_fit_survives_nan_inf invariant 'all finite OR all NaN' panics on Exponential::fit(all zeros) = +Inf — newly-landed harness has too-strict invariant
description:
Review of fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_fit_survives_nan_inf.rs +
p2c007_stats_common.rs on 2026-04-22. These files landed today (e471bab)
implementing plyj.

Bug: fuzz/fuzz_targets/p2c007_stats_common.rs:72-74 defines:

    pub fn all_finite_or_all_nan(values: &[f64]) -> bool {
        values.iter().all(|v| v.is_finite()) || values.iter().all(|v| v.is_nan())
    }

The harness at p2c007_stats_fit_survives_nan_inf.rs:44-45 calls:

    let exponential = Exponential::fit(data);
    assert_params('Exponential', &[exponential.lambda]);

where assert_params uses all_finite_or_all_nan.

Problem: Exponential::fit has a documented intentional return of +Inf
when all data is zero. See test_fit_handles_constant_support at
crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:19699-19700:
    assert!(expon.lambda.is_infinite() && expon.lambda.is_sign_positive())

For data = [0.0, 0.0, 0.0]: Exponential::fit returns {lambda: Inf}.
all_finite_or_all_nan([Inf]):
   values.iter().all(|v| v.is_finite()) -> Inf.is_finite() = false
   values.iter().all(|v| v.is_nan())    -> Inf.is_nan() = false
   Returns false.

The assertion panics. libFuzzer flags this as a crash. Arbitrary can
easily generate [0.0, 0.0, 0.0] (vec of 3 zero f64) as input.

The fix: relax the invariant OR standardize fsci-stats to always
return NaN for degenerate cases instead of Inf. Relaxation is easier:

    pub fn fit_output_classifiable(values: &[f64]) -> bool {
        values.iter().all(|v| v.is_finite()) ||
        values.iter().all(|v| v.is_nan()) ||
        values.iter().all(|v| v.is_finite() || v.is_infinite())
    }

Additional issues in the same file:

1. p2c007_stats_common.rs:43-50 EdgeF64::finite() maps NaN/Inf to
   a hardcoded default value. Same u41o pattern: EdgeF64::Nan variant
   silently becomes default, killing NaN test coverage.

2. p2c007_stats_common.rs:76-82 approx_eq_prob uses inline
   'atol + rtol * scale' math -- inherits i0hm / d09k inf-equality bug.

3. Tolerance CDF_ABS_TOL=1e-4 CDF_REL_TOL=2e-4 at lines 5-6 is
   extremely loose for a cdf(ppf(q))=q identity. 4 orders of magnitude
   off would be needed to trip this.

4. Probability generator 'raw.abs().fract().clamp(1e-9, 1 - 1e-9)'
   at line 62-68 NEVER exercises q exactly 0 or 1, and .fract() on
   large magnitudes produces pseudo-uniform values rather than
   targeting edges.

Fix steps:
1. Relax the invariant for Exponential specifically, OR standardize
   fsci-stats to return NaN for degenerate cases.
2. Replace EdgeF64::finite() with EdgeF64::raw() so NaN/Inf ARE
   exercised.
3. Replace approx_eq_prob with allclose_scalar once i0hm lands.
4. Tighten CDF tolerances to 1e-10 / 1e-8 except where documented
   looser.

Related: plyj (landed but generates this bug), u41o (NaN-to-default
pattern), d09k (inline allclose math), i0hm (inf equality fix),
s6kn / t2wx (fail-closed fit fixes that interact with this invariant).
close_reason: Completed

title: [HIGH] fsci-stats: ContinuousDistribution trait defaults are three-way broken — sf()=1-cdf(x) loses tail precision (same class as mvuj, and only 7 of 85 impls override), fit() default panics via unimplemented!() while entropy/skew/kurt default to silent NaN (asymmetric fail-open), and StatsError has exactly one variant (InvalidArgument(String)) for the whole crate
description:
Review of crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-22.

Three inconsistent and partially-buggy failure patterns in the ContinuousDistribution and DiscreteDistribution trait defaults, touching 85 distribution impls total. Each is independently a bug; together they form a 'distribution trait defaults are broken' theme.

1. sf() DEFAULT LOSES PRECISION IN THE TAIL (same class as mvuj btdtrc).

    Line 52:
        fn sf(&self, x: f64) -> f64 {
            1.0 - self.cdf(x)
        }

    This is the exact pattern the parallel agent just fixed in mvuj for scipy_special_oracle.py's btdtrc lambda. For x far in the right tail, cdf(x) is near 1 and '1 - cdf(x)' loses all significant digits.

    Example (Normal, x=8.0):
        True sf(8)       ~ 6.22e-16 (approx 1 in a quadrillion, one-sided)
        Naive 1-cdf(8)   = 1 - 0.9999999999999994... = ~ 6e-16 (lucky here)
        But for x=10:
        True sf(10)      ~ 7.62e-24
        1 - cdf(10)      = 1 - 1.0 = 0.0    <-- catastrophic

    sf() is overridden in only 7 distribution impls (verified by grep):
        line 400, 495, 809, 1047, 1420, 1567, 52 (trait default itself)
    That means ~78 distribution impls INHERIT the precision-lossy default. For far-tail
    p-values (the primary use of sf in stats), this is wrong-by-default.

    Fix: at minimum, make the default abstract (require each distribution to provide its
    own precision-preserving sf). Ideal: provide sf-via-erfc for Normal, sf-via-exp for
    Exponential, sf-via-gammainc for Gamma/ChiSquared, etc. Same pattern as btdtrc fix.

    logsf() (line 57) builds on sf(), so it inherits the same precision loss.

2. fit() DEFAULT PANICS -- entropy/skewness/kurtosis DEFAULT TO NAN.
   Inconsistent failure-mode strategies for 'method not implemented for this distribution.'

    Line 99-104:
        fn fit(_data: &[f64]) -> Self where Self: Sized {
            unimplemented!("fit method not implemented for this distribution")
        }

    Line 109-111:
        fn entropy(&self) -> f64 {
            f64::NAN
        }

    Line 122-124: skewness() -> f64::NAN
    Line 128-131: kurtosis() -> f64::NAN

    Coverage of explicit overrides (verified by grep):
        fit         overridden in 20 of 85 impls (65 inherit panic)
        entropy     overridden in 15 of 85 impls (70 inherit NaN)
        skewness    overridden in 21 of 85 impls (64 inherit NaN)
        kurtosis    overridden in 21 of 85 impls (64 inherit NaN)

    Consequences:

    a. 65 distributions panic if fit() is called (not caught by Result -- pure panic).
       A user fitting an arbitrary distribution to data cannot handle 'not supported'
       gracefully without catch_unwind.

    b. 70 distributions silently return NaN from entropy(). The user cannot tell the
       difference between 'entropy is legitimately NaN for this distribution' (rare
       but possible) and 'entropy is just unimplemented here.'

    c. The asymmetry itself is a bug: same underlying semantic ('method not
       implemented') expressed two ways. Pick one: either all -> panic, or all ->
       Err/None. SciPy uses NotImplementedError for both cases, which is a raised
       exception -- equivalent to Result<T, Unsupported>, not NaN.

    Fix: make fit() return Result<Self, StatsError::Unsupported>. Make entropy/skew/
    kurtosis return Option<f64> or Result<f64, StatsError>. Both should use the same
    type for 'not supported.' Alternatively, add a sentinel DistributionInfo that
    reports 'has_entropy: bool' per-impl, so callers can check before calling.

3. StatsError has EXACTLY ONE VARIANT.

    Line 23-25:
        pub enum StatsError {
            InvalidArgument(String),
        }

    Same stringly-typed-error anti-pattern as s9tt (fsci-signal SignalError has 3
    variants but uses 1, StatsError has 1 variant total). Every kind of error across
    all of fsci-stats flattens into InvalidArgument(String). Callers cannot
    dispatch on failure mode without parsing the string.

    Fix: expand StatsError with variants for common cases:
        ShapeMismatch { expected: usize, actual: usize },
        NonFiniteInput,
        DataTooSmall { required: usize, got: usize },
        MissingParameter(&'static str),
        Unsupported { operation: &'static str, distribution: &'static str },
        Arithmetic { detail: &'static str },

    The NotImplemented/Unsupported variant is the natural place for the fit() fix in (2).

Combined impact:

- The sf() precision bug is active on every far-tail p-value computation using a
  distribution that doesn't override sf -- the kind of silent statistical error
  that invalidates real-world inference.
- The fit() panic forces defensive catch_unwind in any caller that works with
  multiple distributions generically (e.g., model selection code trying alternative
  distributions).
- The entropy/skew/kurt NaN returns prevent distinguishing 'unimplemented' from
  'undefined' and undermine NaN-propagation detection in downstream pipelines.
- The StatsError single-variant taxonomy prevents caller-side error classification.

Related: mvuj (same sf() precision bug already identified and fixed at the scipy
oracle layer, but not in fsci-stats itself), s9tt (stringly-typed error, same class),
egba (HarnessError stringly-typed), yxml (Debug-format string match), u41o (NaN input
handling in fuzz), m4hu (fit() invariant loss, now strength-5). This bead ties them
together at the fsci-stats trait layer.
close_reason: Completed structured StatsError taxonomy and typed distribution default fallbacks

- chisquare: uniform obs → large p; skewed → small p
- wasserstein: identical distributions → 0; shifted → shift amount

title: [interpolate] CubicSpline standalone + Akima1DInterpolator
description:
## Goal
Implement standalone CubicSpline and Akima1DInterpolator as first-class types, matching scipy.interpolate.CubicSpline and scipy.interpolate.Akima1DInterpolator.

## What to implement
- `CubicSpline`: natural/clamped/not-a-knot/periodic BCs. Stores piecewise polynomial coefficients. Supports `__call__`, `derivative()`, `integrate()`.
- `Akima1DInterpolator`: Akima (1970) method — local cubic interpolation that avoids overshooting. Less oscillation than natural cubic splines for non-smooth data.

## Why this matters
CubicSpline is the most commonly recommended replacement for the deprecated interp1d. Akima fills the gap for users who need robust interpolation without global oscillation (common in engineering/signal data).

## Implementation notes
- CubicSpline: solve tridiagonal system for second derivatives (existing pattern in Interp1d, but now as standalone struct with richer API)
- Akima: compute slopes from local 5-point stencil, then build piecewise Hermite cubics. Does NOT require solving a linear system.
- Both should store PPoly-style coefficients: c[k][i] where c[k][i] * (x - x_i)^(3-k)

## Testing requirements
- CubicSpline: all 4 BC types, derivative continuity at knots, integration test, polynomial reproduction
- CubicSpline periodic BC: f(a) == f(b), f'(a) == f'(b)
- Akima: no overshoot test (step function data), local modification test (changing one point only affects nearby intervals)
- Both: empty/single-point rejection, non-monotone x rejection
- Comparison: CubicSpline and Akima on same data — Akima should have less ringing near discontinuities
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Implemented CubicSplineStandalone (natural/clamped/not-a-knot BCs, derivative, integrate) and Akima1DInterpolator (local, no-overshoot). 12 new tests, 55 total.

title: [optimize] Linear programming: linprog (simplex + interior point)
description:
## Goal
Implement linear programming (LP) solver. LP is one of the most widely used optimization techniques — any problem with linear objective and linear constraints can be solved exactly.

## What to implement
- `linprog(c, A_ub, b_ub, A_eq, b_eq, bounds, method)`: minimize c^T x subject to A_ub @ x <= b_ub, A_eq @ x = b_eq, bounds on x.
  - method="revised_simplex": revised simplex method (standard, exact for LP)
  - method="interior_point" or "highs": interior point method (better for large-scale)
- `LinprogResult`: x (solution), fun (optimal value), slack, success, status, message, nit

## Why this matters
Linear programming appears everywhere: resource allocation, scheduling, network flow, portfolio optimization, supply chain, transportation. It is the foundation for mixed-integer programming and is used in combinatorial optimization.

## Implementation approach
- Revised simplex: standard textbook algorithm with Phase I (find feasible point) and Phase II (optimize). Use Bland anti-cycling rule.
- Convert to standard form: add slack variables for inequalities, handle free variables
- Pivoting with partial pivoting for numerical stability

## Testing requirements
- Simple 2D LP: maximize profit with resource constraints (textbook example)
- Unbounded: detect unbounded objective
- Infeasible: detect infeasible constraints
- Degenerate: LP with redundant constraints
- Integer-like: LP relaxation of an assignment problem
- Bounds: variable bounds properly enforced
- At least 8 tests
close_reason: Implemented linprog with revised simplex (Phase I/II), bounds, equality/inequality constraints, infeasibility detection, 8 tests

title: [e5j6p sub] fsci-stats QMC Halton sequence scaffold
description: Slice of frankenscipy-e5j6p (scipy.stats.qmc parity). Add a qmc submodule to fsci-stats with the Halton low-discrepancy sequence as the first sampler. Halton is base-p radical-inverse per dimension (primes 2,3,5,7,...). Includes (a) HaltonSampler::new(d) constructor, (b) sample(n) returning n×d points in [0,1)^d, (c) star-discrepancy upper-bound check via metamorphic relation: discrepancy(N points) ≤ C·log(N)^d / N (Halton convergence rate), (d) reset/skip API. Defers Sobol, LatinHypercube, PoissonDisk, Owen scrambling, full discrepancy metric — those are subsequent slices on parent bead.

title: [stats] Fisher's exact test for 2x2 tables
description:
## Goal
Implement Fisher's exact test — the gold standard for small-sample contingency tables.

## What to implement
- `fisher_exact(table)`: Two-sided Fisher's exact test for 2x2 contingency tables.
  Returns (odds_ratio, p_value).
- Uses hypergeometric distribution to compute exact p-value.

## Testing
- Known 2x2 table with published p-value
- Extreme tables → very small p
- Independent table → large p
- At least 4 tests
close_reason: Implemented fisher_exact with hypergeometric distribution for exact 2x2 p-values. Computes odds ratio and two-sided p-value. 4 tests, all passing.

title: [HIGH] conformance: contract_table.json audit — 6 of 7 existing tables are DEAD (0 fixture consumption) or WRONG CONTENT; only P2C-003 optimize table is actually consumed, and it has 4 missing entries for 10 cases
description:
Audit of all 7 existing contract_table.json files on 2026-04-22:

Cross-check between contract tables, fixture contract_ref usage, and
loader registration:

| Packet   | Domain      | Contracts | Fixture contract_ref | Status                                 |
|----------|-------------|:---------:|:--------------------:|----------------------------------------|
| P2C-001  | integrate   | 6         | 0/4                  | Dead table: 0 fixture uses            |
| P2C-002  | linalg      | 7         | 0/53                 | Dead table: 0 fixture uses            |
| P2C-003  | optimize    | 4         | 31/39 (4 missing)    | Used but incomplete (4 refs miss)     |
| P2C-004  | sparse      | 9         | 0/27                 | Dead table: 0 fixture uses            |
| P2C-005  | fft         | 3         | 0/56                 | Dead table: 0 fixture uses            |
| P2C-006  | SPECIAL (no file) | - | 232/257           | r575: special contract file missing   |
| P2C-007  | integrate (WRONG) | 6 | 13/29 all miss    | jxi0: wrong domain, copy of P2C-001   |
| P2C-008  | runtime_casp| 3         | 0/15                 | Dead table: 0 fixture uses            |

Net: of 7 contract tables that exist on disk, ONE (P2C-003 optimize)
is actually consumed by a fixture, and even that one has 4 missing
function_name entries vs 8 distinct contract_refs in its fixture. The
other 6 are either dead (no fixture uses), missing (P2C-006), or
content-wrong (P2C-007).

The contract-tolerance architecture (load_optimize_contract_table,
load_special_contract_table, load_array_api_contract_table at
lib.rs:7301-7332) scaffolds three loader functions and three data
files. ZERO loaders functionally serve more than a handful of cases:
- optimize loader: 31/39 cases hit hand-typed atol/rtol, 4 hit
  contract, 4 fall to default
- special loader: 0 hit contract (file missing per r575), 232 cases
  fall to default
- array_api loader: 0 hit contract (file is wrong domain per jxi0),
  13 cases fall to default

For the OTHER 5 families (linalg, sparse, fft, integrate, casp),
contract_table.json files exist but no fixture case references any
contract_ref at all. Completely unused data.

Plus bcv7: 5 more families (spatial, signal, stats, cluster,
integrate's other packet) use contract_ref in fixtures but have no
loader at all. So contract_ref covers a ~100% spread of fixture
files (all use it or all don't) but the RUNTIME CONSUMPTION is zero
or near-zero across the board.

Two distinct classes of waste here:
1. Contract tables emitted for 6 domains that never consume them
   (P2C-001, 002, 004, 005, 008 + bcv7's 5 loader-less families):
   ~35+ entries of human-curated tolerance metadata ignored.
2. Contract tables NOT emitted for the 2 that DO need them:
   P2C-006 missing entirely; P2C-007 has the wrong content.

Ask:
1. Fix jxi0: populate P2C-007/contracts/contract_table.json with real
   arrayapi contracts.
2. Fix r575: create P2C-006/contracts/contract_table.json with special
   contracts.
3. Extend P2C-003 contract_table with entries for trust_exact, toms748,
   ridder, brenth (4 missing contract_refs, 10 affected cases).
4. Either populate the DEAD contract tables (P2C-001/002/004/005/008)
   with meaningful entries AND have fixtures reference them, OR delete
   them as dead configuration.
5. Add the 5 missing loaders per bcv7 (spatial, signal, stats, cluster,
   integrate secondary) with corresponding contract_table.json files.

Related: r575 (P2C-006 missing), jxi0 (P2C-007 wrong content), bcv7
(5 families lack loader), 37ip (tolerance contract gap umbrella),
vh06 (no fixture schema validation catches these).

RuntimeMode definition (crates/fsci-runtime/src/mode.rs:15):
    pub enum RuntimeMode { Strict, Hardened }

Hardened mode is described as: 'Extra safety layer beyond SciPy; adds
finite-check rejection and tighter validation.' It is the mode that
emits audit-ledger entries and gates unsafe numeric inputs. It is the
production deployment mode for fail-closed use cases.

Six entire families have ZERO Hardened test cases:
- stats: no Hardened audit-ledger emission verified for any statistical function
- linalg: no Hardened rejection of NaN in solve/inv/lstsq verified
- arrayapi: no Hardened dtype/shape gate verified
- cluster: no Hardened rejection of NaN-containing data for k-means verified
- signal: no Hardened filter-coefficient validation verified
- fft: no Hardened non-finite input rejection verified

Any regression that admits unsafe inputs in Hardened mode (e.g., a refactor
that silently passes NaN through fsci_linalg::solve without raising) would
slip PAST this conformance suite because no fixture case checks.

Related beads s6kn/t2wx/gwfm/r5tv that I fixed in pass 1 all had Hardened
check requirements per the AGENTS.md fail-closed doctrine. Their fixtures
should specifically add Hardened cases to lock in the behavior.

Ask:
1. For each of the 6 zero-coverage families, add at least 5 Hardened-mode
   cases exercising: NaN input rejection, Inf input rejection, domain-boundary
   rejection, audit-ledger emission presence, pipeline mode-switch behavior.
2. For families with low coverage (<10%), add Hardened variants of the most
   safety-sensitive existing cases (e.g. special gamma at pole, stats NaN
   samples, linalg singular matrix).
3. Minimum target: >=15% Hardened cases per family.
4. Supersedes my bead il3p which incorrectly claimed a 'Relaxed' RuntimeMode
   variant exists -- verified there is no Relaxed variant, only Strict and
   Hardened. Close il3p with reference here.

Related: s6kn, t2wx, gwfm, r5tv (fixed fail-closed bugs without Hardened
coverage in fixture), il3p (invalid assumption).

title: gdtrix incorrectly rejects rate parameter a <= 0.0

However, scipy.special.gdtrix allows a <= 0.0, evaluating gammaincinv(b, p) / a blindly, yielding negative values or infinities (when a=0). This divergence will cause fsci-conformance tests to fail on edge cases. Remove the a <= 0.0 check from gdtrix.

title: [spatial] More distance metrics (hamming, jaccard, canberra, braycurtis) + KDTree extensions
description:
## Goal
Expand distance metric catalog and add KDTree query methods for range searches and neighbor counting.

## What to implement
**Distance metrics:**
- `hamming(u, v)`: fraction of elements that differ (for binary/categorical data)
- `jaccard(u, v)`: Jaccard dissimilarity (for sets/binary vectors)
- `canberra(u, v)`: Σ|u_i-v_i|/(|u_i|+|v_i|) — weighted city-block, sensitive to values near zero
- `braycurtis(u, v)`: Σ|u_i-v_i|/Σ|u_i+v_i| — used in ecology for species abundance
- `mahalanobis(u, v, VI)`: √((u-v)^T VI (u-v)) where VI is inverse covariance matrix
- Add all to DistanceMetric enum and pdist/cdist dispatch

**KDTree extensions:**
- `query_ball_point(point, r)`: find all points within distance r of a query point
- `count_neighbors(other_tree, r)`: count pairs within distance r between two trees
- `sparse_distance_matrix(other_tree, max_distance)`: sparse matrix of distances below threshold

## Testing requirements
- hamming: all-same → 0, all-different → 1
- jaccard: identical sets → 0, disjoint → 1
- mahalanobis: identity covariance → Euclidean
- query_ball_point: returns all and only points within radius
- count_neighbors: consistent with brute-force count
- At least 10 tests
close_reason: Added hamming, jaccard, canberra, braycurtis distance metrics + KDTree query_ball_point and count_neighbors with 11 tests
