title: [HIGH] integrate/quad: quad/quad_vec/cubature epsabs/epsrel NaN pass the < 0.0 reject branch — same fail-open class as i9vw, spread across 3 more sites; adaptive refinement returns converged=false with arbitrary partial sum instead of rejecting at input
description:
Review of crates/fsci-integrate/src/quad.rs on 2026-04-22, companion to frankenscipy-i9vw.

Three additional sites in quad.rs have the same NaN-fail-open pattern as validation.rs:

    Line 228  (quad):           if options.epsabs < 0.0 || options.epsrel < 0.0 { reject }
    Line 282  (quad_vec):        if options.epsabs < 0.0 || options.epsrel < 0.0 { reject }
    Line 1479 (cubature):        if options.atol   < 0.0 || options.rtol   < 0.0 { reject }

Each check fails open for NaN because NaN < 0.0 is false in IEEE 754. A caller passing epsabs=NaN, epsrel=NaN, atol=NaN, or rtol=NaN gets no QuadInvalidTolerance error.

Downstream behavior is then dictated by the adaptive refinement tolerance check at quad.rs:330:

    let tolerance = epsabs.max(epsrel * integral.abs());

With NaN inputs:
  - epsabs = NaN: `NaN.max(x)` in Rust returns x (f64::max treats NaN as smaller).
    So tolerance = epsrel * integral.abs(). If epsrel is also NaN, tolerance = NaN.
  - The recursion continues while `error >= tolerance`. With tolerance = NaN, all
    comparisons return false; loop terminates at the recursion depth limit and
    returns whatever partial sum was accumulated with converged=false.

So with NaN tolerances: quad/quad_vec returns Ok with converged=false and an integral
value that is WHATEVER the adaptive splitter happened to compute at the limit cap.
A caller reading result.integral without checking result.converged gets potentially
meaningful-looking numbers from a request that should have been rejected at input.

Inconsistency in the same file: cubature_bounds_check (line 1474-1496) CORRECTLY
uses `!value.is_finite()` for cubature points at line 1490. The author knew the
right predicate. The shallow `< 0.0` check was used for atol/rtol inconsistently.

Fix (paralleling i9vw):
1. Replace each `options.foo < 0.0` with `!options.foo.is_finite() || options.foo < 0.0`
   (and Inf epsabs/epsrel may be legit -- decide per SciPy's behavior), OR better:
   add explicit is_nan check:

       if options.epsabs.is_nan() || options.epsrel.is_nan() {
           return Err(IntegrateValidationError::QuadInvalidTolerance {
               detail: "tolerances must not be NaN".to_string(),
           });
       }
       if options.epsabs < 0.0 || options.epsrel < 0.0 {
           ... (existing)
       }

2. Add regression tests for each entry point:
    quad(f, 0.0, 1.0, QuadOptions { epsabs: NaN, ... })         => Err
    quad_vec(f, 0.0, 1.0, QuadOptions { epsrel: NaN, ... })     => Err
    cubature(f, a, b, CubatureOptions { atol: NaN, ... })       => Err

3. Consider unifying under a single helper validate_quad_tolerance that's called from all
   three entry points; 3-way duplication of the predicate invites drift.

Related: i9vw (the solve_ivp path; same class of bug in validate_{first_step,max_step,tol}),
95ld (string mode fields for integrate), mhuk (audit ledger gap).
close_reason: All three sites (quad.rs:228 / 282 / 1479) already gate on is_nan() before the < 0.0 strict compare. Grep confirms. Already-fixed, no code change needed.

title: [MEDIUM] conformance: build_replay_command in e2e.rs:665 builds shell command by string interpolation without shell-escaping packet_id/scenario_id/artifact_root — malicious or typo'd fixture ID with shell metacharacters can inject commands when an operator copy-pastes
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/e2e.rs:665-676 on 2026-04-22:

    fn build_replay_command(config, packet_id, scenario_id) -> String {
        format!(
            'cargo run -p fsci-conformance --bin e2e_orchestrator -- --artifact-root {} --packet {} --scenario {}',
            config.artifact_root.display(),
            packet_id,
            scenario_id,
        )
    }

This builds a shell command by direct string interpolation. No
shell escaping is applied to packet_id, scenario_id, or artifact_root.

Call context: build_replay_command is called from run_single_scenario
at line 335 with discovered.packet_id (from fixture JSON) and
scenario.scenario_id (from fixture JSON). These are written into
events.jsonl as the replay_command field, intended for an operator
to copy-paste into a terminal when debugging a failed scenario.

Threat model:

1. Operator copy-paste is TRUSTED -- the operator reads the command
   before running it. So auto-execution is not the attack vector.

2. BUT if packet_id or scenario_id contains shell metacharacters
   (spaces, ';', '|', '&&', '', backticks), the copy-pasted
   command breaks or executes unintended operations. A fixture with:
       scenario_id: 'scenario_01; rm -rf /tmp/stuff'
   produces a replay command that, when pasted, runs the rm -rf.

3. Current fixtures use sanitized IDs (a-z, 0-9, _, -). No malicious
   IDs today. But the runtime contract permits future fixtures to
   contain anything, including accidental shell metacharacters like
   unicode quotes or typos.

4. Fail-closed doctrine demands defenses at serialization layers, not
   just at consumption. A replay command that the operator expects
   to be a SAFE string is actually a partially-attacker-controlled
   payload once written to events.jsonl.

Additional observations:

1. sanitize_path_component at line 678 IS applied to scenario_id for
   the scenario_dir_name (line 315) but NOT for the replay command.
   Two different escaping conventions for the same raw value within
   the same module.

2. artifact_root.display() for non-UTF-8 paths may produce lossy
   output. On Linux paths are bytes, not necessarily UTF-8.

Fix:
1. Emit replay command as a JSON array of arguments, not a shell string:
       replay_command: ['cargo', 'run', '-p', 'fsci-conformance', ...,
                        '--packet', packet_id, '--scenario', scenario_id]
   Tools like jq or Python subprocess.run(..., shell=False) consume
   this safely. Human can manually concatenate with copy-paste awareness.
2. Alternative: use the shlex crate (or manual escaping) to
   shell-quote each argument before interpolation. Requires adding
   shlex as a dependency.
3. Add a test that generates a replay command with a scenario_id
   containing a space + semicolon, asserts the command is either
   escaped correctly OR rejected at emit time.

Related: nub7 (deny_unknown_fields permits typos), gnun (error-string
equality anti-pattern), similar class: trusting string interpolation
of potentially-untrusted content.
close_reason: Shell-quoted replay command args (commit 527585e).

title: [signal] ellipord elliptic filter order
description: scipy.signal.ellipord(wp, ws, gpass, gstop) lowpass. Formula uses complete elliptic integral K. Inline K(m) = π/(2·agm(1, sqrt(1-m))) to avoid pulling fsci-special as a dependency. N = ceil(K(k1²) K(k') / (K(k²) K(k1'))) with k = wp/ws, k1 = sqrt((10^(gpass/10)-1)/(10^(gstop/10)-1)), k' = sqrt(1-k²).

title: [HIGH] conformance: run_smoke + smoke.rs are VACUOUS — run_smoke counts directory entries and checks path existence; never invokes any fsci-* code; CI gate G4 (Adversarial) uses this as its sole check, so G4 passes without testing any scientific logic

        let fixture_count = fs::read_dir(&config.fixture_root)

        HarnessReport {
            suite: 'smoke',
            oracle_present: config.oracle_root.exists(),
            fixture_count,
            strict_mode: config.strict_mode,
        }
    }

What run_smoke actually does:
1. Count entries (files + dirs) in fixture_root directory.
2. Check if oracle_root path exists (doesn't exec python, just filesystem check).
3. Report strict_mode flag.

What run_smoke does NOT do:
- Load any fixture file
- Parse any fixture JSON
- Call any fsci-* function
- Execute any Rust scientific logic
- Invoke any scipy oracle
- Compare any output against any reference

The test at tests/smoke.rs (15 lines):

    fn smoke_report_is_stable() {
        let cfg = HarnessConfig::default_paths();
        let report = run_smoke(&cfg);
        assert_eq!(report.suite, 'smoke');
        assert!(report.fixture_count >= 1);
        assert!(report.oracle_present);
        let fixture_path = cfg.fixture_root.join('smoke_case.json');
        assert!(Path::new(&fixture_path).exists());
    }

Assertions:
1. suite field is literally 'smoke' -- hardcoded at line 1546.
2. fixture_count >= 1 -- fixtures/ has 14+ files; trivially passes.
3. oracle_present == true -- legacy oracle root (legacy_scipy_code/scipy)
   exists in the repo.
4. fixture_path exists -- filesystem presence check.

CI gate G4 Adversarial (per zi0q) runs:
    cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test smoke

G4 pass criteria: filesystem has certain files. That's it.

Impact:
- G4 'Adversarial' gate name promises adversarial-input regression
  testing. Actual: zero adversarial input exercised.
- A critical regression in any fsci-* function that would be caught
  by an actual smoke run passes G4 silently.
- fsci-conformance::run_smoke is exposed as a public function,
  suggesting it has real functionality -- it doesn't.

Combined with 6mdr (golden_journeys writes only), ivg5 (differential
runners are self-check), zi0q (G3/G5/G6 commands don't match names),
G4 is the 4th CI gate that is vacuous or misleading. Only G2 unit
tests + G1 lint + G8 RaptorQ + G7 Schema actually verify anything
(though G7 only validates doc schemas per vh06).

Fix:
1. Replace run_smoke with a minimal real smoke: load FSCI-P2C-001,
   run 1 case through run_differential_validate_tol, assert
   report.fail_count == 0. Now G4 actually runs code.
2. Rename 'Adversarial' to 'SmokeTest' in ci_gates.rs if the
   smoke-test behavior is intentional, OR wire an actual fuzz/
   adversarial corpus per 83b8 (no fuzz CI) + n3au (no ASan/UBSan
   CI) requirements.
3. Delete or refactor run_smoke since its 'count and exist-check'
   behavior is not useful as a public API.

Related: zi0q (CI gate command mismatch), 6mdr (golden writes only),
ivg5 (differential is self-check), 83b8 (no fuzz CI), n3au (no
ASan/UBSan CI). All G-gates under G1 + G2 are misleading or
non-functional.

fsci_signal::taylor diverges materially from scipy.signal.windows.taylor at default args. Verified during z3ni: taylor(8, nbar=4, sll=30, norm=True) — fsci returns -0.120 at index 0 vs scipy 0.279. The fsci impl appears to use a different sign convention or normalization branch in the SLL → eta conversion.

Acceptance:

- 3 fixture cases in P2C-011 covering (n=8, nbar=4, sll=30, norm=true), (n=11, nbar=5, sll=40, norm=false), (n=64, nbar=8, sll=60, norm=true), all bit-exact at rtol=1e-7.

title: EPIC: Optimize Constraints + Global + LinProg
description:
## Background

fsci-opt at 21% coverage implements unconstrained minimizers (BFGS, CG, Powell, NM, L-BFGS-B, Newton-CG), scalar minimization, root finders, curve_fit, least_squares, line_search. Missing (HIGH priority from FEATURE_PARITY.md): Bounds/LinearConstraint/NonlinearConstraint types (needed for constrained minimize), differential_evolution (#3 highest-impact missing function overall), linprog (#4 highest-impact missing), root/fsolve (multivariate root), linear_sum_assignment.

## Goal

Implement constraint types, linprog (simplex + interior point), differential_evolution, and multivariate root finding.

## Success Criteria

1. Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint types integrated with minimize()
2. linprog with revised simplex method
3. differential_evolution global optimizer
4. root() / fsolve() multivariate root finding (hybrd/hybrj methods)
5. linear_sum_assignment (Hungarian algorithm)
6. Unit tests + conformance fixtures for each
7. FEATURE_PARITY.md updated

## Serves Project Goal

differential_evolution and linprog are #3 and #4 on the highest-impact missing list. Constrained optimization is the most-requested missing capability.

title: Implement Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint types
description:
## What

Implement the three constraint types needed for constrained minimize(): Bounds (per-variable box constraints), LinearConstraint (A @ x ≤ b), NonlinearConstraint (lb ≤ c(x) ≤ ub). Wire into existing minimize() dispatcher so that BFGS/L-BFGS-B/etc. can accept constraints.

## Why

Without constraint types, minimize() can only solve unconstrained problems. L-BFGS-B already supports bounds internally but there's no Bounds type to pass them through.

## Implementation Notes

- Bounds: Vec<(Option<f64>, Option<f64>)> with validation
- LinearConstraint: matrix A, vectors lb/ub, keep_feasible flag
- NonlinearConstraint: function c(x), vectors lb/ub, jacobian option
- Wire Bounds into L-BFGS-B minimize path
- For linear/nonlinear constraints: needed by trust-constr or SLSQP (future), but define the types now

## Tests

- Bounds validation (lb ≤ ub)
- minimize with L-BFGS-B + bounds produces constrained solution
- LinearConstraint construction and validation
notes: All three constraint types already implemented in types.rs: Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint with full validation and helper methods
close_reason: already implemented in fsci-opt/types.rs: Bounds, LinearConstraint, NonlinearConstraint

title: Implement linprog (revised simplex)
description:
## What

Implement linprog(c, A_ub, b_ub, A_eq, b_eq, bounds) — linear programming solver. Primary method: revised simplex. Standard form: minimize c^T x subject to A_ub @ x ≤ b_ub, A_eq @ x = b_eq, bounds.

## Why

#4 highest-impact missing function across all of FrankenSciPy. LP is foundational for operations research, economics, and network optimization.

## Implementation Notes

- Revised simplex method: maintain basis inverse factorization, price/ratio test/pivot
- Preprocessing: convert inequality constraints to equalities with slack variables
- Phase I (artificial variables) for finding initial basic feasible solution
- Phase II: optimize
- Return: optimal x, optimal value, status (optimal/infeasible/unbounded), number of iterations
- Consider also implementing interior-point (HiGHS-style) as future enhancement

## Tests

- Classic 2-variable LP → known optimal vertex
- Infeasible problem → status=infeasible
- Unbounded problem → status=unbounded
- Degenerate LP (cycling protection)
- Comparison against hand-solved examples from textbooks
close_reason: linprog already implemented in fsci-opt (verified in reality-check)

title: Implement differential_evolution (global optimizer)
description:
## What

Implement differential_evolution(func, bounds) — stochastic global optimization via differential evolution. Parameters: strategy, maxiter, popsize, tol, mutation, recombination, seed, polish.

## Why

#3 highest-impact missing function across all of FrankenSciPy. The go-to global optimizer when gradient-free optimization is needed over a bounded search space.

## Implementation Notes

- Population: popsize × ndim matrix of candidates within bounds
- Mutation: best1bin, rand1bin, currenttobest1bin strategies
- Crossover: binomial with recombination probability
- Selection: greedy — keep better of trial vs. target
- Polish: optional local minimize (L-BFGS-B or NM) on best result
- Convergence: when std(population values) < tol or maxiter reached
- Seed parameter for reproducibility

## Tests

- Rosenbrock 2D → finds (1,1) within tolerance
- Rastrigin 2D → finds global minimum (not just local)
- Ackley function → global minimum
- Converges faster with polish=true
- Reproducible with fixed seed
close_reason: differential_evolution already implemented in fsci-opt (verified in reality-check)

title: Implement root() and linear_sum_assignment()
description:
## What

1. root(fun, x0, method) — multivariate root finding. Methods: hybr (Powell's hybrid/MINPACK), lm (Levenberg-Marquardt).
2. linear_sum_assignment(cost_matrix) — Hungarian algorithm for optimal assignment.

## Why

root/fsolve is needed for equation solving. linear_sum_assignment is needed for matching problems in combinatorics, tracking, and scheduling.

## Implementation Notes

- root (hybr): Newton's method with dogleg trust-region for robustness. Compute Jacobian via finite differences if not provided.
- root (lm): Levenberg-Marquardt for nonlinear least-squares formulation of root finding.
- linear_sum_assignment: Jonker-Volgenant variant of Hungarian algorithm, O(n³). Handle rectangular cost matrices by padding.

## Tests

- root: f(x) = [x[0]²+x[1]-1, x[0]+x[1]²-1] → two solutions
- linear_sum_assignment: 3×3 known-answer cost matrix
- linear_sum_assignment: rectangular matrix
- linear_sum_assignment: negative costs
notes: Already implemented in fsci-opt

title: [HIGH] conformance: newly-landed scipy_stats_oracle.py dispatches 15 summary-statistics functions but has ZERO distribution method or fit() dispatchers; fsci-stats distribution surface (14 dists × ~10 methods) remains un-oracled
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/python_oracle/scipy_stats_oracle.py (landed 2026-04-22 14:01) on same day, audit 14:05:

Good news (vs cpgl):
- Script exists, is ~7.3 KB, 15 dispatcher arms
- Narrow exception handling at line 165 (ArithmeticError, OverflowError, TypeError, ValueError) -- unlike p3be bare except pattern in other oracles
- scipy_version and numpy_version captured in runtime metadata
- All 30 cases in FSCI-P2C-012_stats_core.json map to the 15 dispatchers (100% fixture coverage)

Gap: the oracle dispatches ONLY summary statistics:
    describe, skew, kurtosis, pearsonr, spearmanr, linregress, ttest_1samp,
    ks_2samp, zscore, sem, iqr, moment, variation, entropy, shapiro (15 functions)

It does NOT dispatch any distribution method. fsci-stats has:
- 14 continuous / discrete distributions (Normal, StudentT, Beta, Gamma, ChiSquared, Exponential, F, Laplace, Logistic, Lognormal, Rayleigh, Maxwell, Pareto, Gumbel, GumbelLeft, Cauchy, Triangular, Weibull, Lomax, ...)
- Each with pdf, cdf, ppf, sf, isf, logpdf, logcdf, mean, var, entropy, fit, rvs
- Plus mstats helpers (count_tied_groups, plotting_positions, etc.)

Total estimated distribution surface: ~140 methods + 20 mstats helpers + other free fns ~= 208 pub fn (per grep -cE '^pub fn ' crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs).

The oracle covers 15 of 208 (7.2%). The fixture FSCI-P2C-012_stats_core.json has ZERO cases for distributions (no 'distribution' or 'method' keys in any case). The 42 e2e_stats.rs scenarios at crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_stats.rs (134 KB) DO exercise distribution chains but are hand-coded, not scipy-oracled.

Critical impact: the Weibull / Lomax / Triangular fit bugs I closed this session (frankenscipy-s6kn, t2wx) and the count_tied_groups fix (gwfm) have no scipy-differential conformance coverage. Future regressions in distribution PDFs, PPFs, or fit paths will not be caught by this oracle.

Ask:
1. Extend scipy_stats_oracle.py with a 'distribution_method' dispatcher:
       if function_name == 'distribution_method':
           dist_name = case['distribution']           # 'norm', 'weibull_min', ...
           method    = case['method']                 # 'pdf', 'cdf', 'ppf', 'fit', ...
           params    = case.get('params', {})
           args      = case['args']
           dist_obj  = getattr(stats, dist_name)(**params)
           result    = getattr(dist_obj, method)(*args)
           ...
2. Extend FSCI-P2C-012_stats_core.json with at least 50 cases exercising:
   - Every distribution's pdf/cdf/ppf at 3 representative x values
   - fit() for the 5 most-used distributions against a canonical sample
   - Mass functions for discrete distributions
   - Edge cases matching the fixed bugs: Weibull.fit([inf, 1]) -> NaN, Triangular.fit([1,1,1]) -> NaN (frankenscipy-s6kn, t2wx regression coverage)
3. Regenerate oracle_capture.json after each fixture expansion.
4. Add a contract_table.json for P2C-012 (cluster fix from r575).

This does not block the existing stats oracle work; it's a scope-expansion follow-up.

Related: cpgl (initial bead -- now landed), r575 (contract table missing for stats), plyj (fuzz targets -- landed), s6kn/t2wx/gwfm (bugs I fixed that lack regression coverage).
close_reason: Added distribution_method dispatcher to scipy_stats_oracle.py. Fixture schema: {function: "distribution_method", distribution: "norm"|"weibull_min"|..., method: "pdf"|"cdf"|"ppf"|"sf"|"fit"|"rvs"|..., params: {loc:, scale:, ...}, args: [...]}. Supports scalar + array method results + fit (class method path). Empirically verified with 6 probe cases (norm.pdf, norm.cdf, norm.ppf, weibull_min.pdf, norm.fit on sample data, expon.cdf on array). Covers all ~140 distribution methods across ~14 distributions via reflection. Follow-up: add ≥50 fixture cases exercising the dispatcher (separate bead).

Verified divergence (8-pt ramp [1..8]):

  - "backward": no scaling.
  - "ortho": each-element factor sqrt(...) per scipy's exact formulas (different per type).

Blocks: mhnr (4-norm coverage).

title: Implement remaining distribution moments: Gompertz, LogLaplace, Mielke, FrechetR
description:
## Context (REVISED after re-audit)
GenLogistic is already implemented. Remaining distributions with NaN moments that have
computable formulas or can use numerical integration:

## Scope
All changes in crates/fsci-stats/src/lib.rs:

1. **Gompertz::mean()** (line ~3425) — Formula: mean = -(1/c)·e^c·Ei(-c) where Ei is the
   exponential integral. Use fsci_special::expi_scalar. Note: for Gompertz distribution
   with parameter c, mean = (1/c)·exp(c)·E₁(c) where E₁ is the exponential integral.
   Available via fsci_special::exp1_scalar or expi_scalar.

2. **Gompertz::var()** (line ~3429) — Compute via numerical integration:
   E[X²] = ∫₀^∞ x²·c·exp(x)·exp(-c·(exp(x)-1)) dx, then var = E[X²] - mean².
   SciPy uses generic numerical integration. We can use adaptive trapezoidal
   quadrature on [0, 20] (tail decays rapidly).

3. **LogLaplace::var()** (line ~3321) — Formula: var = E[X²] - mean² where
   E[X²] = c²/((c-2)(c-1)²) when c > 2. Guard: NaN when c ≤ 2.
   mean = c²/(c²-1) when c > 1 (already implemented).

4. **Mielke::mean()** (line ~3360) — Formula: mean = k·Γ(1+1/k)·Γ(s-1/k)/Γ(s)
   when k·s > 1. Guard: return INFINITY when k·s ≤ 1.
   Need to check Mielke struct fields.

5. **Mielke::var()** (line ~3364) — var = E[X²] - mean² where
   E[X²] = k·Γ(1+2/k)·Γ(s-2/k)/Γ(s) when k·s > 2. Guard: NaN when k·s ≤ 2.

6. **FrechetR::var()** (line ~3518) — Formula: Γ(1-2/c) - Γ(1-1/c)² when c > 2.
   Same structure as InvWeibull. Guard: NaN when c ≤ 2.
   mean = -Γ(1-1/c) when c > 1 (already implemented).

## Dependencies
- fsci_special::expi_scalar (exists at convenience.rs:1115) for Gompertz
- ln_gamma (already used throughout) for LogLaplace, Mielke, FrechetR

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests: Gompertz(0.5), Gompertz(1.0), Gompertz(3.0) mean/var vs SciPy
- Unit tests: LogLaplace(3.0), LogLaplace(5.0) var vs SciPy; LogLaplace(1.5) var = NaN
- Unit tests: Mielke(k,s) mean/var for ≥3 valid param combos vs SciPy
- Unit tests: FrechetR(3.0), FrechetR(5.0) var vs SciPy; FrechetR(1.5) var = NaN
- Edge cases: parameters at boundary of moment existence
- Verify var() ≥ 0 for all valid parameters

## Validation
```
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- gompertz
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- log_laplace
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- mielke
cargo test -p fsci-stats -- frechet
```
close_reason: All distribution moments (Gompertz, LogLaplace, Mielke, FrechetR) are fully implemented with SciPy-verified tests. 6/6 tests pass.

title: PARITY: fsci-interpolate CubicSpline boundary-condition coverage (natural/clamped/not-a-knot/periodic/tuple)
description:
fsci-interpolate ships CubicSpline but only smoke-tests the natural-BC path. scipy.interpolate.CubicSpline supports five boundary-condition modes:
  - natural        (second derivative = 0 at both ends)
  - clamped        (first derivative specified at both ends, e.g. 0)
  - not-a-knot     (default; third derivative continuous across the second knot)
  - periodic       (y, y', y'' match at endpoints)
  - tuple          ((order, value)) per side

Each BC produces a different cubic and a different output value at non-knot evaluation points. A regression in any one of them is invisible if the fixture only tests natural.

Acceptance:
- Add scipy_interpolate_oracle.py (or extend if it exists) with dispatch arm for cubic_spline that takes (x, y, bc_type) and emits sampled values at a finer grid.
- 10+ fixture cases in FSCI-P2C-015 (interpolate) covering each BC + a tuple BC + periodic with sine-data.
- Rust runner: execute_cubic_spline that builds the spline with the case's bc_type and samples at the case's x_eval grid.
- Tolerance contract: 1e-10 absolute for natural / not-a-knot, 1e-9 for clamped / periodic (slight numerical sensitivity), exact endpoint match for periodic.
- One Hardened case: clamped BC with NaN derivative -> expect typed error.

Related: di9p (interpolate has no oracle yet), s5g2 (Hardened sentinel infrastructure).
close_reason: Completed in 8802386

title: [HIGH] conformance: SpecialExpectedOutcome::ErrorKind comparison uses format!('{:?}', kind) string match — breaks when Debug repr changes (enum variants grow fields) AND conflates fixture-schema DomainError with genuine domain violations
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/lib.rs:10997-11006 on 2026-04-22:

    (SpecialExpectedOutcome::ErrorKind { error }, Err(actual)) => {
        let observed_kind = format!('{:?}', actual.kind);
        let pass = observed_kind == *error;
        ...
    }

Problems:

1. Debug-format coupling. Rust's Debug repr is NOT a stable string contract. If FsciSpecialErrorKind::DomainError grows to DomainError { detail: String }, the Debug output changes from 'DomainError' to 'DomainError { detail: "..." }'. All 10 adversarial cases (case.category == 'adversarial') using expected.kind='error_kind' + expected.error='DomainError' silently flip pass -> fail without any actual behavior change.

2. Inconsistency with Class comparator. At 10987-10989 the ValueClass comparison uses typed enum equality (observed_class == *class). The ErrorKind comparator should mirror this pattern: compare typed enums, not Debug strings. The Class path is the right pattern; the ErrorKind path regresses to the string-compare anti-pattern documented in gnun.

3. Masks fixture-schema bugs. Per frankenscipy-04jy, special_invalid_fixture_error mints FsciSpecialErrorKind::DomainError for ANY argument-count mismatch (191 sites). So a fixture like:
       case_id: 'adv_gamma_pole_hardened_error'
       function: 'gamma'
       args: [-2.0, 3.0, 1.0]   # typo: should be [-2.0]
       category: 'adversarial'
       expected: {kind: 'error_kind', error: 'DomainError'}
   would:
   - enter execute_special_case at 10509 (actually gamma takes 1 arg, not covered there; use a 1-arg fn)
   - actual handler sees wrong arity, returns special_invalid_fixture_error (DomainError kind)
   - compare_special_case_differential sees observed_kind='DomainError'
   - matches expected='DomainError' -> PASS
   - claim 'verified gamma fails closed on pole' even though we hit an arity check before touching gamma
   The test is lying about what it verified.

Fix:
1. Introduce a typed comparison path. Parse expected.error into FsciSpecialErrorKind (perhaps via FromStr or a lookup table) and compare enum vs enum:
       let expected_kind: FsciSpecialErrorKind = error.parse()
           .map_err(|_| (false, format!('unknown error kind: {error}'), None, None))?;
       let pass = actual.kind == expected_kind;
2. Separate fixture-schema errors from domain errors (ties to 04jy) -- a fixture-schema error should make the case UNJUDGEABLE (per ihex), not match a DomainError expectation.
3. Mirror the Class comparator pattern at 10987.

Related: gnun (error-string equality anti-pattern), 04jy (DomainError overloaded for fixture-schema), ihex (both-errored = pass amplification), tfd7 (metamorphic self-consistency is strength-4 without strength-1 cross-check).

- Tolerance: 1e-12 for closed-form windows (rectangular, triangular, hann); 1e-7 for iterative ones (chebwin Dolph-Chebyshev).
- Three Hardened cases: kaiser with NaN beta, gaussian with NaN sigma, exponential with NaN tau -> typed error.

title: [b6z3m sub] fsci-special jn_zeros first k zeros of J_n
description: Slice of frankenscipy-b6z3m. Add scipy.special.jn_zeros(n, k) returning first k positive zeros of Bessel J_n. Uses McMahon's asymptotic expansion j_{n,k} ≈ (k + n/2 - 1/4)π · (1 - (4n²-1)/(8μ²) + ...) where μ = (4k + 2n - 1)π/4 as initial guess, then refines via bisection in a 0.5-radius bracket (same robustness pattern as ai_zeros). Add metamorphic relations: J_n(zero) ≈ 0, zeros are positive and monotonically increasing, first zero of J_0 ≈ 2.4048.

title: [MEDIUM] fsci-linalg: solve_circulant silently zeros out near-singular FFT-space modes (|λ|²<1e-30) instead of raising — produces wrong 'solution' that doesn't satisfy Cx=b for singular/ill-conditioned circulants; scipy.linalg.solve_circulant raises LinAlgError by default via singular='raise'; no input finiteness check either
description:
Review of crates/fsci-linalg/src/lib.rs on 2026-04-23.

solve_circulant (line 4308-4348) silently zeros out near-singular frequency
components rather than raising LinAlgError, producing wrong answers for
singular or ill-conditioned circulant systems.

Problematic code (line 4330-4342):

    let fft_x: Vec<(f64, f64)> = fft_b
        .iter()
        .zip(fft_c.iter())
        .map(|(&(br, bi), &(cr, ci))| {
            let denom = cr * cr + ci * ci;
            if denom < 1e-30 {
                (0.0, 0.0)                                  // <-- silent singularity mask
            } else {
                ((br * cr + bi * ci) / denom, (bi * cr - br * ci) / denom)
            }
        })
        .collect();

Analysis:

A circulant matrix C has FFT-space diagonalization C = F^* diag(λ) F, where
λ_k = FFT(c)[k]. Solving Cx = b becomes x̂_k = b̂_k / λ_k.

When λ_k ≈ 0, the system is singular (or nearly so). The code silently sets
x̂_k = 0 in that case, producing a 'solution' that fails to satisfy Cx = b
when b̂_k ≠ 0. This is a classic 'return wrong answer silently on singular
input' bug.

Scipy comparison:
- scipy.linalg.solve_circulant has two extra parameters:
    tol: float, default 1e-16 * max(|eigenvalues|)
    singular: 'raise' (default) or 'lstsq'
  With singular='raise', raises scipy.linalg.LinAlgError when any |λ_k|
  falls below tol.
  With singular='lstsq', computes least-squares solution using 0 for the
  null-space modes.
- Our implementation is silently a 'lstsq'-mode solver with tol fixed at
  sqrt(1e-30) ≈ 3e-16 of the eigenvalue magnitude squared. No 'raise' mode,
  no user-configurable tolerance.

Impact:

1. Users migrating from scipy see different behavior: scipy raises on
   singular input (default); our impl silently returns a wrong solution.

2. The 'wrong' solution satisfies C * x ≈ P_range(C) * b (projection of b
   onto range of C), not Cx = b. For users expecting exact solution, this
   is a correctness bug.

3. Detection requires checking ||Cx - b|| afterward, which users shouldn't
   have to do for a solve function that's advertised as a 'solver.'

4. NaN fall-open: if cr or ci is NaN (which shouldn't happen given earlier
   NaN validation at the solve entry points, but solve_circulant doesn't
   have any own validation), denom = NaN, NaN < 1e-30 is false, takes
   else branch, divides by NaN, output NaN silently. No input validation
   on the circulant vector c.

Additional concerns in solve_circulant:

a. No validation that c is finite. Line 4308 is:
       pub fn solve_circulant(c: &[f64], b: &[f64]) -> Result<Vec<f64>, LinalgError>
   No mode parameter. No check_finite parameter. No Hardened-mode path.
   Other solve functions (solve, solve_triangular, solve_banded) take
   SolveOptions with mode and check_finite. Inconsistent API.

b. Divergence threshold 1e-30 is on denom = |λ|² not |λ|. So the cutoff
   is at |λ| ≈ 1e-15, close to machine epsilon for 1.0-scale matrices.
   For small-scale matrices this is far too permissive; for large-scale
   it's too strict. Scipy scales tol by max |eigenvalue|.

Fix:

1. Add a `tol` parameter (or use a default based on max|eigenvalue|):
       let max_lambda_sq = fft_c.iter().map(|&(cr, ci)| cr*cr + ci*ci).fold(0.0, f64::max);
       let tol = 1e-28 * max_lambda_sq;  // or parameterized

2. Detect singularity and raise:
       if denom < tol {
           if br*br + bi*bi > tol * tol * max_b_sq {
               return Err(LinalgError::SingularMatrix);
           }
           (0.0, 0.0)  // only if b̂_k is also zero
       }

3. Add SolveOptions-based variant for scipy-parity mode control:
       pub fn solve_circulant_with_options(..., options: SolveOptions) -> ...

4. Validate c and b for finiteness at function entry.

Regression tests:
    let c = vec![1.0, -2.0, 1.0];  // circulant with eigenvalue 0 (λ[1] = 1 - 2*e^{2πi/3} + e^{4πi/3} = 0 for certain c)
    let b = vec![1.0, 0.0, 0.0];   // b̂ not in range of C
    let x = solve_circulant(&c, &b);
    assert!(x.is_err());  // currently returns Ok with wrong answer

Related: l15l (procrustes silent divergence via Newton-Schulz), wofb
(fsci-stats sf precision loss), n240 (cluster metrics silent 0.0),
42z1 (mmread DoS), 1dpl (RBF silent NaN). Pattern: functions that should
signal computational failure but instead return degraded-quality output.
close_reason: Fail-closed on singular solve_circulant (commit 818d02d).

title: drss follow-up: nuttall coefficient fix + 9 multi-arg windows

drss closed with 3 1-arg windows (parzen/bohman/lanczos) in a127748. Remaining work:
1. Fix fsci_signal::nuttall_window coefficients to match scipy's minimum-4-term Blackman-Harris convention.
2. Extend SignalCase args schema to accept param vector for multi-arg windows.
3. Add 9 more windows: tukey (alpha), gaussian (std, sym), exponential (center, tau, sym), kaiser_bessel_derived (beta, sym), general_cosine (coeffs, sym), general_hamming (alpha), chebwin (at), taylor (nbar, sll, norm, sym), dpss (NW, Kmax).

Acceptance: each new window gets at least 1 fixture case bit-exact against scipy.signal.windows at rtol=1e-9.
notes: Fixed via eeb8a54. dpss dispatch wired with (n, nw, sym) -> Approximate normalization, kmax=None single taper. Two fixture cases at rtol=1e-9. All 12 multi-arg windows from acceptance now have fixtures: parzen, bohman, lanczos, nuttall, tukey, gaussian, exponential, kaiser_bessel_derived, general_cosine, general_hamming, chebwin, taylor, dpss.

title: [HIGH] conformance CI: G3 runs golden_journeys+schema_validation (not differential); G5 runs only e2e_linalg (1 of 16 e2e); G6 runs only perf_linalg (1 of 8 perf); G3 and G7 duplicate schema_validation
description:
Review of crates/fsci-conformance/src/ci_gates.rs:60-82 on 2026-04-22:

Gate command audit:
  G1Lint       -> cargo fmt --check, cargo clippy (OK)
  G2UnitTests  -> cargo test --workspace (broad, OK)
  G3Conformance-> golden_journeys + schema_validation       <-- but named 'Differential Conformance'
  G4Adversarial-> smoke                                     <-- smoke test != adversarial
  G5E2e        -> e2e_linalg                                <-- 1 of 16 e2e_*.rs files
  G6Performance-> perf_linalg                               <-- 1 of 8 perf_*.rs files
  G7Schema     -> schema_validation                         <-- DUPLICATE of G3's second cmd
  G8RaptorQ    -> raptorq_proofs

Test files available but NOT invoked by G5/G6/G7:
  e2e_*.rs     (15 additional): e2e_casp, e2e_cluster, e2e_fft, e2e_interpolate,
                e2e_io, e2e_ivp, e2e_ndimage, e2e_optimize, e2e_orchestrator,
                e2e_signal, e2e_sparse, e2e_spatial, e2e_special, e2e_stats
  perf_*.rs    (7 additional): perf_arrayapi, perf_casp, perf_fft, perf_ivp,
                perf_optimize, perf_sparse, perf_special
  diff_*.rs    (2 entirely uncovered): diff_fft, diff_sparse
  evidence_*.rs (11 entirely uncovered): evidence_p2c001..p2c008, p2c012, p2c016

Impact:
1. G3 is MISLABELED. It runs golden_journeys + schema_validation; it does NOT run the differential_<family>_quota_and_structured_logs tests in lib.rs. The gate title 'Differential Conformance' over-promises.
2. G5 E2E gate only covers linalg e2e. Any e2e regression in signal, stats, sparse, fft, special, integrate, cluster, spatial, ndimage, optimize, casp, interpolate, io, ivp is NOT caught by this gate.
3. G6 Performance gate similarly covers only linalg perf.
4. G7 Schema is a duplicate of G3's second command. Running the same test twice wastes budget.
5. G2UnitTests at 'cargo test --workspace' does pick up cargo tests from every crate -- so technically the e2e_* tests ARE invoked through G2, but they run under G2's 300s budget (G5 has 900s). An e2e test that takes >300s to run would fail G2 but silently pass if directly run via G5.

Ask:
1. Split G5 into G5a..G5n per e2e test file; OR invoke

title: Add E2E conformance tests for stub-remediation: moments, interpolation, BDF Newton
description:
## Context
As stubs are remediated across fsci-stats, fsci-ndimage, and fsci-integrate, we need
E2E conformance tests to verify correctness against SciPy reference values with full
forensic logging per ARTIFACT_TOPOLOGY.md.

## Scope

### 1. E2E stats moments verification (e2e_stats.rs scenarios 11-14)
Add scenarios to crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_stats.rs:

- **e2e_011_closed_form_moments**: Verify Burr12, InvWeibull, TukeyLambda,
  GenHalfLogistic mean/var against SciPy reference values (multiple parameter combos)
- **e2e_012_special_function_moments**: Verify GenLogistic, Gompertz mean/var
  against SciPy reference values
- **e2e_013_piecewise_integral_moments**: Verify CrystalBall, Argus, ExponWeibull
  mean/var against SciPy reference values
- **e2e_014_tukey_lambda_analytical**: Verify TukeyLambda analytical PDF values
  and CDF/PPF roundtrip accuracy

### 2. E2E ndimage interpolation (new test file)
Create crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_ndimage.rs:

- **e2e_001_shift_interpolation**: Compare shift with orders 0,1,3 to SciPy
- **e2e_002_zoom_interpolation**: Compare zoom with orders 0,1,3 to SciPy
- **e2e_003_rotate_interpolation**: Compare rotate with orders 0,1,3 to SciPy
- **e2e_004_map_coordinates**: Compare map_coordinates with orders 0,1,3 to SciPy

### 3. E2E BDF Newton (add to existing integrate tests)
- **e2e_bdf_stiff_convergence**: Van der Pol μ=1000, verify convergence with
  Newton where functional iteration would fail

### Output format
All E2E tests emit topology-compliant artifacts:
- events.jsonl (one JSON line per step)
- summary.json (pass/fail + replay command)
- Under fixtures/artifacts/FSCI-P2C-{NNN}/e2e/runs/{run_id}/{scenario_id}/

## Testing Requirements
- All new scenarios must pass with all_pass = true
- Forensic logs must capture: parameter values, computed moments, reference values,
  absolute error, relative error, tolerance used, pass/fail per step
- Reference values should be hardcoded from SciPy (not computed at test time)

## Dependencies
Depends on: all other beads in this series (moments, interpolation, BDF)

## Validation
```
cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test e2e_stats
cargo test -p fsci-conformance --test e2e_ndimage
cargo test -p fsci-conformance
```
close_reason: All E2E conformance tests already implemented and passing: e2e_stats 011-014 (4 tests), e2e_ndimage (1 test with 4 steps), e2e_ivp BDF Newton (1 test). All pass.

title: Support free variables in linprog and milp
description:
Evidence:
- [crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs:534) rejects `linprog` bounds with `lo = -inf` and explicitly says free variables are not yet supported.
- [crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs](/data/projects/frankenscipy/crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs:876) applies the same restriction to `milp`.
- SciPy's `linprog`/`milp` surfaces accept unbounded and free variables via `None`/infinite bounds.

Scope:
- Add variable-splitting support for fully free variables and correct handling for one-sided infinite bounds.
- Thread the transformed variables through `linprog` Phase I/II extraction and branch-and-bound node bounds in `milp`.
- Add regression tests for `(-inf, inf)`, `(-inf, hi)`, and mixed bounded/free-variable problems.
- Verify the solution reconstruction and objective value match SciPy semantics.

Suggested files:
- `crates/fsci-opt/src/lib.rs`
- `crates/fsci-conformance/tests/e2e_optimize.rs`
close_reason: Already implemented on HEAD

title: [parity-gap] jn_zeros / yn_zeros fail for moderate-to-large order n
description:
scipy.special.jn_zeros(100, 1) returns ~108.836; fsci jn_zeros(100, 1) returns ~120.8 (the McMahon asymptotic guess), missing the true zero by ~12 units. Same defect in yn_zeros.

Root cause: McMahon's asymptotic expansion (DLMF 10.21.19) μ=(4k+2n−1)π/4 is accurate when k>>n, not when n is moderate-to-large. For n=100, k=1, McMahon gives initial ≈ 120.83 but the true first zero of J_100 is ≈ 108.84. The current adaptive bracket-expansion (radius 1, then 8× ±0.3) only reaches ±3.4 from the initial — far too narrow to catch the true zero. The function falls through to 'keep the asymptotic guess' (crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs:3160), silently returning a wrong answer.

Repro:
  let zeros = fsci_special::jn_zeros(100, 1);
  let val = fsci_special::jn(100, zeros[0]);  // |val| should be near 0
  // Currently val ≈ J_100(120.83) which is large, NOT a zero.

Existing tests miss this because they only cover n ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3} (crates/fsci-special/src/bessel.rs:4155-4168).

Fix options:
  1) Replace McMahon with Olver/Tricomi expansion (DLMF 10.21.40):
       j_{n,1} ≈ n + 1.85575 n^(1/3) + 1.03315 n^(−1/3) − ...
     valid for the small-k, large-n regime.
  2) Use a piecewise initial guess: McMahon when k > n/2, Olver otherwise.
  3) Widen the adaptive expansion to ±20 with verification that the bracket
     brackets a sign change before bisection.

Acceptance: jn(n, jn_zeros(n, k)[k-1]) is < 1e-6 in absolute value for at
least n ∈ {0, 5, 20, 100} and k ∈ {1, 5, 20}; same for yn_zeros.

title: [b6z3m sub] fsci-special riccati_jn + riccati_yn
description: Slice of frankenscipy-b6z3m. Add scipy.special.riccati_jn(n, x) and scipy.special.riccati_yn(n, x) returning (S_0..=S_n, S'_0..=S'_n) where S_k(x) = x·j_k(x) (riccati_jn) or C_k(x) = -x·y_k(x) (riccati_yn). Closed-form recurrences: S_0 = sin(x), S'_0 = cos(x), then S_{k+1} from spherical Bessel recurrence and S'_k = -k S_k/x + S_{k-1} for k ≥ 1. Add metamorphic relations: S_0(x) = sin(x), C_0(x) = cos(x), recurrence consistency.

title: [signal] lp2bs_zpk lowpass-to-bandstop ZPK transform
description: scipy.signal.lp2bs_zpk. Equivalent to compose lp2hp then bandpass-style split: zhp = bw/(2z), pair-split via sqrt(zhp² - wo²), append degree zeros at ±i·wo, gain k * Re(prod(-z)/prod(-p)).

title: [io] reject non-32-bit IEEE float WAV reads
description:
## What

wav_read currently treats IEEE float (audio_format=3) with 8/16/24-bit sample sizes as PCM, which silently mis-decodes malformed WAV headers.

## Fix

- Reject audio_format=3 unless bits_per_sample == 32 (the only float width we implement).
- Add a regression test for a float-format header with 16-bit samples.

## Why

Avoids mis-decoding malformed files and makes behavior explicit for unsupported float widths.
close_reason: Rejected IEEE float WAV with non-32-bit samples and added regression test
